clauses.title: General environment — five-class exposure classification (and abrasive exposure pointer)
clauses.notes: Bundles the one-sentence 8.2.2.2 'Abrasive' clause inline (final paragraph). The abrasive exposure pointer is too short and too distinct from the five-level scheme to warrant a separate chunk; treating it as an addendum to 8.2.2.1 keeps the rules-layer query-by-exposure surface coherent.

clauses.title: Environmental exposure conditions — five-level classification
clauses.caption: Five-level classification of environmental exposure for concrete in service, indexed by the exposure category and described by the placing/service environment that maps to that category. Each row gives the category name (column 1) and one or more bullet-style descriptions of the exposure conditions that classify into that category (column 2). The exposure-class axis defined here is the categorical input feeding into Table 5 (cement/grade lookup, clause 8.2.4.1), Table 16 (nominal cover for durability, clause 26.4.2), and clause 35.3.2 (surface-crack-width limits 0.3 / 0.2 / 0.1 mm). Referenced from clauses 8.2.2.1 and 35.3.2.
rows: Concrete surfaces protected against weather or aggressive conditions, except those situated in coastal area.

Concrete surfaces sheltered from severe rain or freezing whilst wet. | Concrete exposed to condensation and rain. | Concrete continuously under water. | Concrete in contact or buried under non-aggressive soil / ground water. | Concrete surfaces sheltered from saturated salt air in coastal area.

Concrete surfaces exposed to severe rain, alternate wetting and drying, or occasional freezing whilst wet, or severe condensation. | Concrete completely immersed in sea water. | Concrete exposed to coastal environment.
Concrete surfaces exposed to sea water spray, corrosive fumes, or severe freezing conditions whilst wet. | Concrete in contact with or buried under aggressive sub-soil / ground water.
Surface of members in tidal zone. | Members in direct contact with liquid / solid aggressive chemicals.
clauses.note: The exposure-condition descriptions in column 2 are presented as pipe-separated bullets within each row, preserving the multi-condition nature of the printed table (e.g. 'Moderate' has five conditions any of which classifies into the Moderate category). A project-specific exposure assessment maps the actual service environment onto exactly one of the five categories; where multiple bullets apply to a member, the highest-severity category that has at least one matching bullet governs.
clauses.nl_summary: Categorical lookup of environmental exposure class. Five categories (Mild, Moderate, Severe, Very severe, Extreme); each category is defined by one or more environment descriptions. The class is the categorical input axis for downstream durability-cum-design rules (cement/grade per Table 5; cover per Table 16; crack-width per 35.3.2).

variables.description: exposure class — output of the classification; one of {mild, moderate, severe, very_severe, extreme}
free-text description of the service environment — input to the classification, mapped to the most severe category that has a matching bullet

clauses.notes: No amendments touch Table 3. Body verbatim per the printed code; the multi-bullet condition descriptions are normalized into pipe-separated cells preserving the original groupings. The mapping commentary in the note field (about highest-severity-bullet-governs) is editorial inference about how a downstream classification rule should resolve when multiple bullets apply — IS 456 does not state this explicitly, but it is the necessary disambiguation rule for any rules-layer implementation.

clauses.title: Fire resistance — designed to flame penetration, heat transmission, and failure resistance; time-rated per IS 1641; fire safety details per IS 1642
clauses.text:
A structure or structural element required to have fire resistance shall be designed to possess an appropriate degree of resistance to: flame penetration, heat transmission, and failure under the action of fire. The fire resistance of a structural element is expressed in terms of time in hours, in accordance with IS 1641. The fire resistance of concrete elements depends upon details of member size, cover to steel reinforcement, detailing, and type of aggregate (normal weight or light weight) used in the concrete.

Essential requirements of fire safety of buildings with respect to details of construction are given in IS 1642.

The rule structure has two layers. The first layer is the performance requirement — every element designated as fire-resisting must withstand three failure modes (flame, heat, structural failure) for a stated number of hours. The hours are determined by the building category and fire-zone classification of IS 1641. The second layer is the construction-detail requirement, delegated to IS 1642. Within IS 456, the cover requirements that satisfy the fire-resistance ratings are given in clause 26.4.3 and Table 16A (encoded in Session 1) for nominal cover, and Fig 1 (this batch) for minimum member dimensions.
clauses.notes: Amendment No. 5 (July 2019) substituted the fourth sentence of this clause with the new sentence about IS 1642 (encoded here as the second paragraph): 'Essential requirements of fire safety of buildings with respect to details of construction are given in IS 1642.' Pre-amendment text had a more discursive reference to general fire protection. The amendment also brings the IS 1642 reference in the Annex A list up to date (1642 : 2013). All cross-references encoded reflect post-amendment state.

clauses.title: Fire resistance — minimum cover per 26.4.3 and minimum member dimensions per Fig 1, for normal-weight aggregate concrete
clauses.text:
Minimum requirements of concrete cover and member dimensions for normal-weight aggregate concrete members, so as to have the required fire resistance, shall be in accordance with clause 26.4.3 and Fig 1 respectively.

The rule splits the fire-resistance dimensioning into two parallel lookups, both indexed by the required fire-resistance rating in hours. Cover-to-steel is looked up in clause 26.4.3 (encoded in Session 1) and its Table 16A, which gives nominal cover values for beams, slabs, ribs, columns, and walls for fire ratings from 0.5 to 4 hours. Member dimensions — beam width, rib width, floor (slab) thickness, column dimension (with three exposure cases), wall thickness (with three reinforcement-ratio cases) — are looked up in Fig 1 of this Section. Both lookups apply only to normal-weight aggregate concrete; for light-weight aggregate, specialist literature is needed (per clause 21.4).
clauses.notes: Pairs Fig 1 (this batch) with Table 16A from Session 1. The 'normal-weight aggregate' restriction is procedurally important — the values in Fig 1 and Table 16A are calibrated for conventional sand-and-gravel or crushed-stone concrete; light-weight aggregate concrete needs separate treatment per 21.4.

clauses.title: Minimum dimensions of reinforced concrete members for fire resistance
clauses.nl_caption: Composite figure with three diagram bands and one numerical table. The diagram bands are: BEAMS (rectangular cross-section labelled b, and T-section labelled b), SLABS (solid slab labelled D, and rib/waffle slab labelled D and bw), and COLUMNS (square column fully exposed labelled b and D, circular column fully exposed labelled b and D, square column with 50-percent exposure labelled b, and square column with one face exposed labelled b). The numerical table beneath the diagrams gives minimum dimensions in millimetres for six fire-resistance ratings (0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, and 4 hours) across nine column categories: Beam Width b, Rib Width bw, Floor Thickness D, Column Dimension Fully Exposed, Column Dimension 50-percent Exposed, Column Dimension One Face Exposed, and three Wall Thickness columns indexed by the percentage of steel reinforcement (p < 0.4 percent, 0.4 percent ≤ p ≤ 1 percent, p > 1 percent). For 0.5 hour rating the values are 200, 125, 75, 150, 125, 100, 150, 100, 100 millimetres respectively. For 1 hour: 200, 125, 95, 200, 160, 120, 150, 120, 100. For 1.5 hours: 200, 125, 110, 250, 200, 140, 175, 140, 100. For 2 hours: 200, 125, 125, 300, 200, 160, (blank — see notes), 160, 100. For 3 hours: 240, 150, 150, 400, 300, 200, (blank), 200, 150. For 4 hours: 280, 175, 170, 450, 350, 240, (blank), 240, 180. Two NOTES accompany the table: NOTE 1 — these minimum dimensions relate specifically to the covers given in Table 16A; NOTE 2 — p is the percentage of steel reinforcement. The blank cells in the lowest wall-reinforcement column (p < 0.4 percent) for fire ratings 2 hours and above indicate that wall design at that low reinforcement ratio is not tabulated for those higher fire ratings — the next column (0.4 to 1 percent) gives the minimum for those cases.
clauses.notes: Pairs operationally with Table 16A from Session 1 (cl_26_4_3 / table_16a_nominal_cover_fire_resistance). Together they form the two-axis fire-design lookup: cover from Table 16A, member dimensions from Fig 1. The blank cells for wall thickness at p < 0.4 percent for fire ratings 2 hours and above are reproduced from the source PDF; the operational rule is that walls with reinforcement below 0.4 percent do not have tabulated minimum thicknesses for those higher fire ratings — designers must use the 0.4-to-1-percent column, or step up the reinforcement, or refer to specialist literature per 21.4. The embedded table within this figure is a candidate for separate encoding as table_fig1_fire_dimensions in a migration-time pre-processing step (synthetic table number per the unnumbered-embedded-table convention), but is captured here in nl_caption for now since the figure-table tie is geometrically inseparable in the source.

clauses.title: Fire resistance — reinforcement detailing shall reflect the changing structural action under fire and ensure adequate ties, bonds, and anchorages
clauses.text:
The reinforcement detailing should reflect the changing pattern of the structural action and ensure that both individual elements and the structure as a whole contain adequate support, ties, bonds, and anchorages for the required fire resistance.

Under fire exposure the structural action of a member changes — bending moments redistribute as the steel temperature rises and its yield strength drops, shear capacity reduces with the concrete cover spalling away, and tension or compression zones may relocate. Detailing that is adequate for room-temperature design may become inadequate for the fire-deformed state. The clause therefore mandates that the detailing — ties, lap splices, anchorage lengths — be checked against the fire-induced action redistribution, not only against the ambient design action. The 'adequate support, ties, bonds, and anchorages' wording is the operational hook for ensuring the detailing remains effective when individual bars are weakened.
clauses.notes: Amendment No. 1 (June 2001) substituted 'action' for 'section' in line 2 — the pre-amendment printed text reads 'changing pattern of the structural section' which is a typographical error; the amended reading 'changing pattern of the structural action' is conceptually correct, since it is the structural action (the bending-moment, shear-force, and axial-force distribution under fire) that changes, not the section. This chunk encodes the post-amendment reading directly.

clauses.title: Fire resistance — additional measures (fire-resistant finishes, false ceilings, sacrificial steel) where nominal cover exceeds 40 mm beams / 35 mm slabs
clauses.text:
Additional measures such as application of fire-resistant finishes, provision of fire-resistant false ceilings, and sacrificial steel in the tensile zone shall be adopted in case the nominal cover required exceeds 40 mm for beams and 35 mm for slabs — to give protection against spalling.

The rule addresses a counter-intuitive failure mode: very thick concrete cover, while providing more thermal insulation to the bars, becomes itself prone to explosive spalling at high temperatures because the entrained moisture in the cover layer turns to steam and bursts the surface concrete outwards. Once the cover spalls, the bars are exposed to fire directly and the protection benefit is lost. The 40-millimetre threshold for beams and 35-millimetre threshold for slabs marks the cover values above which spalling risk becomes design-relevant. Three additional-protection routes are sanctioned: (a) fire-resistant finishes applied to the surface, (b) fire-resistant false ceilings providing a secondary thermal barrier, and (c) sacrificial steel placed in the tensile zone — additional bars whose loss does not threaten the load-carrying capacity.
clauses.notes: The 40 mm / 35 mm thresholds are linked to spalling risk, not to fire resistance directly — they identify the cover range above which additional non-structural fire protection becomes necessary. The 'sacrificial steel' route is a particularly Indian convention not always found in other national codes.

clauses.title: Fire resistance — specialist literature for cases not covered by Fig 1 or Table 16A
clauses.text:
Specialist literature may be referred to for determining the fire resistance of structures that have not been covered in Fig 1 or in Table 16A.

The clause is the formal escape hatch for cases outside the tabulated values: light-weight aggregate concrete, prestressed elements, very thin members, unusual cross-section shapes, or fire ratings beyond the 4-hour limit of the tables. Such cases require recourse to specialist literature — typically standardised fire-resistance test data from accredited laboratories, or computational fire-engineering analysis using validated thermal-mechanical models. The clause does not prescribe what counts as specialist literature; the responsibility lies with the engineer-in-charge to demonstrate that the alternative source is competent.
clauses.notes: Procedural fallback for non-tabulated cases — light-weight aggregate, prestressed, unusual shapes, ratings above 4 hours.

clauses.title: Nominal cover to meet durability requirements
clauses.caption: Minimum nominal concrete cover (mm) to all reinforcement including links, indexed by exposure category. Rows are the five exposure categories (mild, moderate, severe, very severe, extreme); the single data column gives the cover-not-less-than value. Three notes apply: (1) for main reinforcement up to 12 mm diameter bar under mild exposure, the nominal cover may be reduced by 5 mm; (2) actual concrete cover should not deviate from the required nominal cover by more than +10 mm and 0 mm (i.e. the −10 mm tolerance is not allowed — cover must never go below the table value); (3) for severe and very severe exposure, a 5 mm reduction may be made where the concrete grade is M35 and above. Referenced from clause 26.4.2 (Session 1, already authored as cl_26_4_2_durability_cover).
headers: Nominal Concrete Cover not Less Than (mm)
clauses.note: Note 1: For main reinforcement up to 12 mm diameter for mild exposure the nominal cover may be reduced by 5 mm. Note 2: Unless specified otherwise, actual concrete cover should not deviate from the required nominal cover by more than +10 mm (no negative tolerance permitted). Note 3: For exposure conditions 'severe' and 'very severe', a reduction of 5 mm may be made where concrete grade is M35 and above.

Three modifier rules adjust the base value for small-diameter bars in mild exposure, for high-strength concrete in severe/very-severe exposure, and bound the tolerance asymmetrically.

clauses.title: Control of deflection — final and post-partition serviceability limits
clauses.text:
The deflection of a structure or any part of it shall not adversely affect the appearance or efficiency of the structure, its finishes, or its partitions. The clause sets two numerical limits on flexural-member deflection, applied independently — both must be satisfied:

(a) Final deflection — the total deflection including the effects of all loads, temperature, creep, and shrinkage, measured from the as-cast level of the supports of floors, roofs and other horizontal members, shall not normally exceed span/250.

(b) Deflection occurring after erection of partitions and application of finishes — that is, the deflection from the time the partitions or finishes are installed onward, including subsequent loading effects, temperature change, and time-dependent creep and shrinkage — shall not normally exceed the smaller of span/350 and 20 mm. The 20 mm absolute cap dominates for long spans (above 7 m) where span/350 would exceed it.

The word 'normally' carries the relaxation that the engineer may permit exceedance where the structure's specific use, finish system, or partition type tolerates more, but the relaxation must be defended. Conversely tighter limits may be required for sensitive cladding or for serviceability checks under particular loading.

The limits set here are the targets that the span-to-depth ratios of clause 23.2.1 are deemed to satisfy when those ratios are met. If span-to-depth ratios are exceeded, deflection must be explicitly calculated per Annex C and shown to comply with both (a) and (b) above. Clause 42.1 (limit state of serviceability: deflection) refers back to both this clause and 23.2.1 as the controlling limits for flexural members under limit state design.

clauses.title: Span-to-effective-depth ratios deemed to satisfy deflection limits
clauses.text:
The vertical deflection limits of clause 23.2 may generally be assumed satisfied — that is, no explicit deflection calculation is required — when the span-to-effective-depth ratio of the flexural member does not exceed the value obtained by the following procedure:

(a) Basic values, applicable for spans up to 10 m:
  • Cantilever: 7
  • Simply supported: 20
  • Continuous: 26

(b) For spans above 10 m, the basic values in (a) are multiplied by the factor 10/L (where L is the span in metres). The exception is the cantilever case: for cantilever spans greater than 10 m, the simple multiplication does not apply and a full deflection calculation per Annex C is required instead.

(c) The result of (a) or (b) is then multiplied by a modification factor obtained from Fig. 4, which is a function of the percentage of tension reinforcement and the service-load steel stress f_s. Five curves are given on Fig. 4 for f_s = 120, 145, 190, 240, and 290 N/mm². The service stress is computed per the formula printed below Fig. 4: f_s = 0.58·f_y·(A_st,required / A_st,provided).

(d) If compression reinforcement is provided, the result of (c) is further multiplied by a modification factor obtained from Fig. 5, which is a function of the percentage of compression reinforcement.

(e) For flanged beams (T-beams or L-beams), the basic values of (a) or (b) are also multiplied by the reduction factor of Fig. 6, which is a function of the ratio of web width to flange width b_w/b_f. Additionally, the reinforcement percentages used for entering Fig. 4 and Fig. 5 in steps (c) and (d) shall be computed on an area equal to b_f·d (the effective-flange-width based area), not on b_w·d.

NOTE 1: For slabs spanning in two directions, the shorter of the two spans is used when calculating the span-to-effective-depth ratio.

NOTE 2: For two-way slabs of shorter span up to 3.5 m with mild steel reinforcement (Fe 250) and loading class up to 3 kN/m², the following span-to-overall-depth ratios may be used instead of the span-to-effective-depth procedure: simply supported 35, continuous 40. For high-strength deformed bars of grade Fe 415, these values are multiplied by 0.8 (giving 28 and 32 respectively).

The steps are multiplicative: the final permissible ratio is the basic value of (a)/(b) times the modification factors from Fig. 4 (always), Fig. 5 (if compression steel present), and Fig. 6 (if flanged). Any one factor below 1.0 reduces the permissible ratio; any factor above 1.0 increases it.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Permissible span-to-effective-depth ratio = basic value × Fig4_factor × Fig5_factor × Fig6_factor. Basic values: cantilever 7, simply supported 20, continuous 26 (for spans ≤ 10 m); above 10 m, multiply by 10/L (L in metres), except cantilever (use Annex C). Fig4_factor (tension steel %, f_s) always applied. Fig5_factor (compression steel %) applied only if compression steel present. Fig6_factor (b_w/b_f) applied only for flanged sections, and the reinforcement % for Fig 4 and Fig 5 is then computed on area b_f·d. Service steel stress for Fig 4 lookup: f_s = 0.58·f_y·(A_st,req / A_st,prov).

clauses.title: Modification factor for tension reinforcement
clauses.caption: Five-curve graph giving the deflection-control modification factor k_t as a function of two inputs: the percentage of tension reinforcement (on the x-axis, 0 to 3.0) and the service-load steel stress f_s (the parameter labelling each curve). Curves are drawn for f_s = 120, 145, 190, 240, and 290 N/mm². The y-axis shows the modification factor (range approximately 0.4 to 2.0). Factor decreases with increasing tension reinforcement percentage and with increasing service stress; the curve f_s = 145 corresponds approximately to mild-steel-Fe-250 conditions, and f_s = 240 to Fe-415 working stress. To enter the chart, compute f_s = 0.58·f_y·(A_st,required / A_st,provided) and interpolate between the bracketing curves for that f_s value at the section's tension steel percentage.

parameters.description: service-load steel stress f_s = 0.58·f_y·(A_st,req / A_st,prov); selects which curve to use, with linear interpolation between adjacent curves when the computed f_s falls between two tabulated values

curves.name: f_s = 120 N/mm²
curves.description: Modification factor curve for service steel stress f_s = 120 N/mm² — the uppermost curve on the chart (highest modification factor at any given tension reinforcement percentage).

curves.name: f_s = 145 N/mm²
curves.description: Modification factor curve for service steel stress f_s = 145 N/mm² — approximately corresponds to mild steel (Fe 250) under typical working-stress design conditions.

curves.name: f_s = 190 N/mm²
curves.description: Modification factor curve for service steel stress f_s = 190 N/mm² — intermediate stress level.

curves.name: f_s = 240 N/mm²
curves.description: Modification factor curve for service steel stress f_s = 240 N/mm² — approximately corresponds to Fe 415 high-strength deformed bars at full service stress.

curves.name: f_s = 290 N/mm²
curves.description: Modification factor curve for service steel stress f_s = 290 N/mm² — the lowermost curve on the chart (lowest modification factor at any given tension reinforcement percentage), corresponds to high-grade reinforcement worked close to its service-stress capacity.

percentage of tension reinforcement at the section — input x-axis

clauses.notes: Points pending WebPlotDigitizer digitisation in Session 4. All five curve point arrays intentionally empty until digitised. The parameters block records the five f_s values (120, 145, 190, 240, 290 N/mm²) as the curve-selection axis. Once digitised, each curve becomes a piecewise-linear lookup over percentage tension reinforcement, with linear interpolation between adjacent f_s curves for intermediate stress values.

clauses.title: Modification factor for compression reinforcement
clauses.caption: Single-curve graph giving the deflection-control modification factor k_c as a function of the percentage of compression reinforcement (x-axis, 0 to 2.5 percent). Y-axis is the modification factor (range approximately 1.00 to 1.50). The curve rises monotonically from 1.0 at zero compression steel — i.e. no enhancement when no compression reinforcement is present — to approximately 1.50 at the highest compression-steel percentages shown. The factor is multiplicative on the span-to-depth ratio from step (a)/(b) of clause 23.2.1, and is applied only when compression reinforcement is actually provided. For flanged sections, the compression-steel percentage entered on the x-axis is computed on the area b_f·d per clause 23.2.1(e).

curves.name: Compression-steel modification factor
curves.description: Single curve, monotonically increasing from approximately (0, 1.0) to approximately (2.5, 1.50). Represents the additional permissible span-to-depth ratio earned by providing compression steel.

variables.description: modification factor for compression reinforcement (output of the chart)

clauses.title: Reduction factor for ratios of span to effective depth for flanged beams
clauses.caption: Single-curve graph giving the deflection-control reduction factor k_f for flanged beams (T-beams and L-beams) as a function of the ratio of web width to flange width b_w/b_f (x-axis, 0 to 1.0). Y-axis is the reduction factor (range approximately 0.70 to 1.00). The curve rises from approximately 0.75 at b_w/b_f = 0.2 to 1.0 at b_w/b_f = 1.0, reflecting that as the section approaches a rectangular shape (web width approaching flange width), no flanged-section reduction is needed. The factor is multiplicative on the span-to-depth ratio from step (a)/(b) of clause 23.2.1, and is applied only for flanged sections; k_f = 1.0 for rectangular beams. For rectangular sections, this chart is skipped entirely.
