clauses.title: Slenderness limits for beams to ensure lateral stability
clauses.text:
Beams must be proportioned so that the unbraced length between lateral restraints (or between a free end and the nearest lateral restraint, for a cantilever) is bounded as follows:

For a simply supported or continuous beam, the clear distance between lateral restraints shall not exceed the smaller of 60·b and 250·b²/d, where b is the breadth of the compression face of the beam measured midway between the lateral restraints, and d is the effective depth.

For a cantilever, the clear distance from the free end of the cantilever to the lateral restraint shall not exceed the smaller of 25·b and 100·b²/d, where b is the breadth of the compression face midway between the lateral restraints (or, for a cantilever, at the location appropriate to the unbraced length).

These limits guard against lateral-torsional buckling of the beam in flexure. The dual criterion — a constant multiple of b and a quadratic-in-b/d term — captures two distinct buckling regimes: 60·b (and 25·b for cantilever) governs short, wide beams; the 250·b²/d (and 100·b²/d) term governs slender beams with high effective depth where d-dependent reduction is needed.

The controlling limit at any specific geometry is whichever produces the smaller permissible unbraced length. For a typical b = 300 mm and d = 600 mm beam, 60·b = 18 000 mm and 250·b²/d = 37 500 mm, so 60·b governs; for the same width but d = 1500 mm, 60·b = 18 000 mm and 250·b²/d = 15 000 mm, so the b²/d term governs.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Slenderness limits for lateral-torsional buckling: simply supported or continuous beam → clear distance between lateral restraints ≤ min(60·b, 250·b²/d); cantilever → free-end-to-restraint distance ≤ min(25·b, 100·b²/d). b is breadth of compression face midway between restraints; d is effective depth.

variables.description: clear distance between adjacent lateral restraints (simply supported or continuous beam); or distance from free end of cantilever to nearest lateral restraint
breadth of the compression face of the beam measured midway between the lateral restraints
effective depth of the beam per clause 23.0

clauses.title: Clear distance between bars
clauses.caption: Maximum permitted clear distance between adjacent parallel tension reinforcement bars in flexural members, in mm. Rows are indexed by characteristic strength of reinforcement f_y (250, 415, 500 N/mm²) — discrete grades. Columns are indexed by percentage redistribution of moments at the section being considered (−30 percent to +30 percent, in steps of 15 percent) — the continuous interpolation axis. Negative redistribution percentages indicate that moment has been shifted away from the section (reducing demand and permitting wider spacing); positive percentages indicate moment shifted to the section (increasing demand and tightening the spacing requirement, except that in this table positive redistribution does not tighten the limit below the 0% column for f_y = 250 — those values are clamped at 300 mm). Referenced from clause 26.3.3 (max distance between bars in tension), Session 1.
clauses.note: Values are clear distances between bars in millimetres. The note in the printed table states: spacings are not applicable to members in particularly aggressive environments unless, in the calculation of the moment of resistance, f_y has been limited to 300 N/mm² in limit state design and σ_st has been limited to 165 N/mm² in working stress design. This caveat is a hard gate — for an aggressive environment, the table cannot be used unless the section is also designed at a reduced steel stress.
clauses.nl_summary: Lookup table giving the maximum permitted clear distance between tension reinforcement bars, by f_y (discrete row axis: 250, 415, 500) and redistribution percentage (continuous column axis: −30 to +30 percent). Use with linear interpolation across columns for intermediate redistribution percentages.

variables.description: maximum permitted clear distance between adjacent parallel tension bars (output)
characteristic yield strength of tension reinforcement — input discrete row axis: 250, 415, or 500

percentage redistribution of moments to or from the section under consideration — input continuous column axis from −30 to +30 percent; positive means moment shifted to this section, negative means moment shifted away

clauses.notes: Interpolation explicitly column-wise: redistribution_pct is the continuous axis (rows are discrete f_y grades). This is the first table in the corpus to use the new linear_between_columns enum value introduced in Session 3. Cross-batch forward ref to cl_22_7_redistribution_of_moments — the redistribution percentage used as the column-axis lookup is governed by 22.7 (procedural admissibility) and 37.1.1 (maximum percentage). Cross-session forward ref to cl_26_3_3_max_distance_tension (Session 1) — the parent clause that mandates use of this table for flexural members in normal internal/external conditions.

clauses.title: General — reinforcement type, bundling, earthquake detailing reference
clauses.text: The main reinforcement in any structural member must be of a single type and grade. It is acceptable, however, to use one type or grade for the main steel and a different one for secondary reinforcement in the same member. Bars may be placed singly, in pairs in contact, or in bundles of three or four; per 26.1.1, bundled bars must be enclosed inside stirrups or ties and must be tied together so they remain grouped, and bars larger than 32 mm in diameter cannot be bundled except when they are used in columns. Per 26.1.1, congestion of reinforcement should also be avoided in detailing; available techniques include careful selection of bar diameter and grade and the bundling of reinforcement where appropriate. Per 26.1.2, where earthquake-resistant detailing is applicable, the requirements of IS 13920 (and IS 4326) must also be considered alongside this code.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Single grade for main steel in a member. Bundles up to 4 bars, all bundled bars enclosed in stirrups/ties; bars > 32 mm bundled only in columns. Earthquake detailing per IS 13920.
clauses.notes: Amendment No. 3 (August 2007), p. 42, clause 26.1.1: adds a sentence on congestion avoidance at end of 26.1.1. Pre-amendment printed text had no congestion-of-reinforcement guidance. Post-amendment reading — encoded here in paraphrased form — recommends avoiding congestion during detailing and lists techniques such as careful selection of bar diameter and grade and the bundling of reinforcement where appropriate.

clauses.title: Development length of bars
clauses.text: Per 26.2 (intro), the calculated tension or compression in any bar at any section must be developed on each side of that section by an adequate development length, end anchorage, or a combination. The development length L_d is given by L_d = (φ · σ_s) / (4 · τ_bd), where φ is the nominal bar diameter, σ_s is the bar stress at the section under design loads, and τ_bd is the design bond stress from clause 26.2.1.1. Three notes apply: (1) for tension reinforcement, the anchorage value of any standard hooks is already included in this length; (2) for bars of non-circular cross-section, the length must still develop the full bar stress through bond; (3) for plain cement concrete of M15 grade with nominal reinforcement, the design bond stress may be taken as 1.0 N/mm².
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Development length L_d equals bar diameter times bar stress at design load divided by four times the design bond stress from clause 26.2.1.1.

variables.description: development length of the bar (output) — length required on each side of a section to develop the bar's calculated tension or compression through bond
nominal diameter of the bar being developed (input)
stress in the bar at the section under design loads (input); for LSM use 0.87·f_y at yield
design bond stress between concrete and the bar surface (input, from clause 26.2.1.1, depending on concrete grade and bar surface — plain or deformed, tension or compression)

clauses.notes: Amendment No. 4 (May 2013), p. 42, clause 26.2.1, Note 2: inserts a new Note 3 at end of clause 26.2.1. Pre-amendment printed text had two notes only (hooks and non-circular cross-section). Post-amendment reading — encoded here in paraphrased form — adds a Note 3 stating that for plain cement concrete of M15 grade with nominal reinforcement, the design bond stress may be taken as 1.0 N/mm². The Amendment No. 4 insertion does not change the L_d formula itself; the variables block was authored against the unchanged formula during Pass 1B.

clauses.caption: Design bond stress τ_bd (N/mm²) for plain bars in tension under the limit state method, by concrete grade. Embedded inline in clause 26.2.1.1; not numbered as a 'Table N' in IS 456.
clauses.note: Multipliers given in the surrounding clause text: (a) for deformed bars conforming to IS 1786, multiply the tabulated values by 1.60; (b) for bars in compression, multiply the corresponding tension value by 1.25 (so a deformed bar in compression takes 1.60 × 1.25 = 2.00 × the plain-tension value). Working stress method bond stresses are given in Annex B-2.1, not here.
clauses.nl_summary: Design bond stress τ_bd for plain bars in tension under LSM, indexed by concrete grade: 1.2 (M20), 1.4 (M25), 1.5 (M30), 1.7 (M35), 1.9 (M40+) N/mm². Apply ×1.60 for deformed bars (IS 1786) and ×1.25 for compression. τ_bd feeds the development length formula L_d = φ·σ_s / (4·τ_bd) in clause 26.2.1.

clauses.title: Development length of bundled bars in contact
clauses.text: When bars are bundled in contact, the development length of each individual bar in the bundle must be increased over the value computed for a single bar in clause 26.2.1. The increase is 10 percent for two bars in contact, 20 percent for three bars in contact, and 33 percent for four bars in contact. The increased length applies to every bar in the bundle, not only to the outer bars.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Bundled-bar development length multiplier on the single-bar L_d: 1.10 for 2-bar bundle, 1.20 for 3-bar bundle, 1.33 for 4-bar bundle.

clauses.title: Bond stress for fusion-bonded epoxy coated bars — 80 % factor
clauses.text: For fusion-bonded epoxy coated deformed bars, the design bond stress shall be taken as 80 percent of the corresponding values tabulated in clause 26.2.1.1 (see table_26_2_1_1_bond_stress). The 80 percent factor reflects the reduced bond capacity of the epoxy coating relative to a bare deformed-bar surface; it is applied multiplicatively to the table value for the relevant concrete grade and with any other applicable per-clause modifiers (the 1.60 deformed-bar uplift and the 1.25 compression-side multiplier, both from the surrounding 26.2.1.1 text). The amendment is silent on the strict order of application of these multipliers; engineering convention is to apply them multiplicatively in any order since the result is identical.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: FBE-coated deformed bars: design bond stress is 80 percent of the value from the 26.2.1.1 bond-stress table (after the standard deformed-bar uplift, if applicable). For a Fe-deformed bar in M25 tension: τ_bd = 1.4 × 1.60 × 0.80 = 1.79 N/mm² (vs 2.24 N/mm² without the FBE factor).

variables.description: effective design bond stress for fusion-bonded epoxy coated deformed bars (output) — 80 percent of the corresponding deformed-bar bond stress
design bond stress for deformed bars (input) — the table value from 26.2.1.1 (plain-bar baseline) with the 1.60 deformed-bar uplift applied; the FBE 80 percent factor is then applied on top of this

clauses.notes:
Inserted by Amendment No. 4 (May 2013), p. 43, clause 26.2.1.1 paragraph 2: appends a new sentence at end of paragraph 2 stating that for fusion-bonded epoxy coated deformed bars, design bond stress values shall be taken as 80 percent of the values given in the bond-stress table (the table embedded in clause 26.2.1.1, encoded in this corpus as table_26_2_1_1_bond_stress). The Amendment No. 4 amendment is silent on the order of application of this 80 percent factor relative to the 1.60 deformed-bar uplift and the 1.25 compression-side multiplier given in the surrounding clause text; engineering convention treats them as commutative multipliers, so the application order does not affect the numerical result.

Architectural decision (Phase 2 sub-pass 2C): this Amendment No. 4 content was authored as a new clause chunk rather than as a notes-field entry on table_26_2_1_1_bond_stress so that the FBE rule is queryable by clause ID in the rules-layer (an FBE design check can look up cl_26_2_1_3_fbe_coated_bars directly without table-notes-field parsing). The chunk ID's `_3` suffix is corpus-positional (next available after the existing `_1` table and `_2` bundled-bars siblings); the actual IS 456 sub-clause is 26.2.1.1 (carried in the `clause` field). No IS 456 clause 26.2.1.3 exists.

Variables block authored during Phase 2 sub-pass 2C for this new chunk.

clauses.title: Anchoring bars in tension — deformed bars, bends, hooks
clauses.text: Per 26.2.2 (intro), every reinforcing bar must be anchored on each side of any section it crosses. For bars in tension, deformed bars do not require special end anchorages provided the development length requirement of 26.2.1 is met; for plain bars in tension, hooks should normally be provided. Bends and hooks must conform to IS 2502. The anchorage value of a bend is taken as 4 times the bar diameter for each 45 degrees of bend, capped at a maximum of 16 times the bar diameter regardless of how many 45 degree increments are present. The anchorage value of a standard U-type hook is fixed at 16 times the bar diameter.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Tension anchorage: deformed bars need only L_d (no special end anchor); plain bars normally need hooks. Bend anchorage = 4φ per 45° (max 16φ); standard U-hook anchorage = 16φ.

clauses.title: Anchoring bars in compression
clauses.text: For a bar in compression, the anchorage length of a straight bar must equal the development length of bars in compression as specified in clause 26.2.1 (which uses the τ_bd value increased by 25 percent for compression). Where hooks, bends, or straight extensions beyond bends are provided on a compression bar, only their projected length — not their geometric path length — may be counted toward the development length. In other words, hooks and bends contribute no extra anchorage value in compression beyond their physical projection.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Compression anchorage = full L_d (compression). Hooks/bends count only by their projected (straight) length, not by an anchorage-value rule.

clauses.title: Mechanical anchorage devices
clauses.text: A mechanical anchor or other proprietary device may be used in place of a standard development length, provided two conditions are satisfied: it must be capable of developing the full strength of the bar, and it must not damage the surrounding concrete. Use of such a device requires the approval of the engineer-in-charge.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Mechanical/proprietary anchors are allowed if they develop full bar strength without damaging concrete and have engineer-in-charge approval.

clauses.title: Anchorage of shear reinforcement — inclined bars and stirrup hooks
clauses.text: Anchorage of shear reinforcement has two cases. (a) For inclined bars, the development length is the same as for tension bars, but the length must be measured from a defined origin: in the tension zone, from the end of the sloping/inclined portion of the bar; in the compression zone, from the mid-depth of the beam. (b) For stirrups and other secondary transverse ties, the code overrides general development-length rules and deems the anchorage adequate when the bar is bent around a longitudinal bar of at least its own diameter and continues past the curve by a length depending on the bend angle: 8 bar diameters past a 90° bend, 6 bar diameters past a 135° bend, or 4 bar diameters past a 180° bend.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Inclined shear bars: L_d as for tension, measured from end of incline (tension zone) or mid-depth (compression zone). Stirrup hook: 90° → 8φ tail, 135° → 6φ tail, 180° → 4φ tail; bend around a long. bar ≥ stirrup diameter.

clauses.title: Bearing stress at bends — internal radius check
clauses.text: When a bar is bent, the concrete inside the bend resists a radial bearing force. For bends and hooks that conform to IS 2502, no bearing-stress check is required. For any other bend, compute the bearing stress as F_bt / (r · φ), where F_bt is the design tensile force in the bar (or in a bundle, the equivalent force), r is the internal radius of the bend, and φ is the bar diameter (or the equivalent diameter of a bundle on an area basis). Under the limit state method this stress shall not exceed 1.5·f_ck / (1 + 2φ/a), where f_ck is the characteristic cube strength and a is the centre-to-centre spacing of bars perpendicular to the plane of the bend; for a bar adjacent to a face of the member, a is taken as the cover plus φ. The working stress method imposes a separate, lower limit — see Annex B.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Inside-bend bearing stress F_bt/(r·φ) must not exceed 1.5·f_ck/(1 + 2φ/a) under LSM. Skip the check entirely for IS 2502-conforming bends/hooks. WSM uses a different (lower) limit per Annex B.

variables.description: bearing stress on concrete inside the bend (computed; must not exceed the LSM cap)
design tensile force in the bar (or equivalent force in a bundle) at the bend (input)
internal radius of the bend (input)
diameter of the bar (or equivalent bundle diameter computed on an area basis) (input)
characteristic cube compressive strength of concrete (input)
centre-to-centre spacing of bars perpendicular to the plane of the bend (input); for a bar adjacent to a face of the member, taken as cover + φ

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Splitting force from change in reinforcement direction
clauses.text: When tension or compression reinforcement changes direction inside a member, the change generates a resultant force. If that resultant acts outward — that is, away from the concrete core, tending to spall or split the cover — additional links or stirrups must be provided to take up the force. The clause also gives a related design preference: a tension bar bent at a re-entrant angle (where the resultant acts outward) should be avoided in the first place rather than detailed with extra ties.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: If a bend in a tension or compression bar pushes outward on the cover, add links/stirrups to resist the splitting force; bent tension bars at re-entrant angles should be avoided.

clauses.title: Curtailment of tension reinforcement — extension past the cut-off point
clauses.text: Per 26.2.3 (intro), this rule governs how far flexural tension steel must run beyond the section where it is no longer needed. Once a bar is no longer required to resist flexure, it must continue for an additional length equal to the greater of the effective depth d of the member or 12 times the bar diameter (12φ). The only exceptions are at a simple support and at the end of a cantilever, where this extension does not apply. The point at which a bar is 'no longer required for flexure' is defined in the attached note as the section where the moment of resistance, computed using only the bars that continue past the section, equals the design moment. In addition to this extension, sub-clauses 26.2.3.2 through 26.2.3.5 must also be satisfied.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Tension bar must continue past its theoretical cut-off by max(d, 12φ); not required at simple supports or cantilever tips. Cut-off point is where the continuing bars alone resist the design moment.

variables.description: effective depth of the flexural member (input)
nominal diameter of the bar being curtailed (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Termination of flexural reinforcement in a tension zone — three permitted conditions
clauses.text: A flexural bar may not be terminated inside a tension zone unless at least one of three conditions is met. (a) The factored shear at the cut-off point is at most two-thirds of the section's permitted shear capacity, including the contribution of any web reinforcement. (b) Excess stirrup area beyond what shear and torsion already require is provided over a distance from the cut-off equal to three-quarters of the effective depth (0.75d); the excess area must satisfy A_v ≥ 0.4·b·s / f_y, where b is the beam breadth, s the stirrup spacing, and f_y the characteristic yield strength of reinforcement in N/mm², and the resulting spacing must not exceed d / (8·β_b), where β_b is the ratio of the area of bars cut-off to the total area of bars at the section. (c) For bars of diameter 36 mm or smaller, the bars that continue past the cut-off provide at least twice the area required for flexure at that section, and the factored shear is at most three-quarters of the permitted shear.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Cannot end a flexural bar in a tension zone unless: (a) shear ≤ 2/3 capacity, OR (b) excess stirrups A_v ≥ 0.4·b·s/f_y over 0.75d with spacing ≤ d/(8·β_b), OR (c) for ≤36 mm bars, continuing bars give 2× flexural area AND shear ≤ 3/4 capacity.

variables.description: additional stirrup area required (output) — placed over a distance 0.75·d from the cut-off, in excess of what shear and torsion already demand
breadth of the beam (or breadth of the web b_w for flanged sections) (input)
spacing of the stirrups along the member (input)
characteristic yield strength of the stirrup reinforcement (input)
effective depth of the beam (input)
ratio of the area of bars cut-off at the section to the total area of bars at that section (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Positive-moment reinforcement extension and diameter limit at supports
clauses.text: Three rules govern positive-moment steel near supports. (a) At least one-third of the positive-moment reinforcement in a simply supported member, and at least one-fourth in a continuous member, must extend along the same face of the member into the support for a length equal to L_d / 3. (b) When the flexural member belongs to the primary lateral-load-resisting system, this reinforcement must additionally be anchored to develop its full design tensile stress at the face of the support. (c) At simple supports and at points of inflection, the diameter of the positive-moment tension reinforcement is restricted so that L_d (computed per 26.2.1 using the design stress f_d) does not exceed M_1 / V + L_0, where M_1 is the moment of resistance of the section assuming all reinforcement at that section is stressed to f_d, V is the factored shear at the section, and L_0 is the available anchorage beyond the centre of a simple support (or, at a point of inflection, is limited to the greater of d and 12φ); f_d equals 0.87·f_y under LSM. The value of M_1 / V may be increased by 30 % when the bar ends are confined by a compressive reaction.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Positive-moment steel: extend ≥1/3 (simple) or ≥1/4 (continuous) into support over L_d/3; for primary lateral system, anchor for full design stress at support face. At simple supports and inflection points, bar diameter limited so L_d ≤ M_1/V + L_0 (M_1/V × 1.3 if confined by compression reaction).

variables.description: development length of the bar at the design stress f_d (computed per 26.2.1)
moment of resistance of the section assuming all reinforcement at the section is stressed to the design stress f_d (input)
shear force at the section due to factored design loads (input)
available anchorage length beyond the centre of a simple support (input); at a point of inflection, limited to max(d, 12·φ)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Negative-moment reinforcement extension past the point of inflection
clauses.text: At least one-third of the total reinforcement provided to resist negative moment at a support must continue past the point of inflection. The extension distance from the point of inflection must be at least the greatest of three quantities: the effective depth d of the member, twelve times the bar diameter (12φ), or one-sixteenth of the clear span. This rule applies in addition to the basic curtailment extension in 26.2.3.1.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: At least one-third of negative-moment steel at a support continues past the point of inflection by max(d, 12φ, clear span / 16).

variables.description: effective depth of the flexural member (input)
nominal diameter of the bar (input)
clear span of the member (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Curtailment of bundled bars — staggered termination
clauses.text: When bars are bundled, individual bars in the bundle must be terminated at staggered points rather than all at the same section. The minimum stagger distance between successive termination points is 40 times the bar diameter (40φ). The only exception is a bundle that runs into and stops at a support — in that case, the bars in the bundle may end at the same section.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Bundled bars must terminate at staggered points spaced ≥ 40φ apart, except when the bundle ends at a support.

variables.description: nominal diameter of the bar being terminated in the bundle (input); the stagger distance between successive bundle terminations is at least 40·φ

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Special members — end anchorage where bar stress is not proportional to moment
clauses.text: In ordinary flexural members the tensile bar stress varies in step with the bending moment, so the development-length rules of 26.2.1 are sufficient on their own. The clause flags four classes of members where this proportionality does not hold and adequate end anchorage must therefore be provided explicitly: sloped, stepped, or tapered footings; brackets (corbels); deep beams; and any flexural member in which the tension reinforcement is not parallel to the compression face. In each of these, the geometry concentrates bond demand near the bar end rather than spreading it along the bar, so end anchorage is treated as a separate detailing check beyond the standard L_d.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Provide explicit end anchorage on tension steel in flexural members where bar stress isn't proportional to moment: sloped/stepped/tapered footings, corbels, deep beams, and members with non-parallel tension steel and compression face.

clauses.title: Lap splices — applicability, lengths, modifiers, and special cases
clauses.text: Per 26.2.5 (intro), splices should be located away from sections of maximum stress, staggered, and not placed where the bending moment exceeds 50 % of the moment of resistance; not more than half the bars at any section may be spliced. (a) Lap splices shall not be used for bars larger than 32 mm; bars larger than 32 mm shall be welded (see 12.4) or mechanically spliced. (b) Splices count as staggered if their centre-to-centre spacing is at least 1.3 times the lap length from (c). (c) The lap length, including the anchorage value of any hooks, is the greater of L_d (per 26.2.1) and 30φ for bars in flexural tension, and the greater of 2·L_d and 30φ for bars in direct tension; the straight portion of the lap must be at least 15φ or 200 mm, whichever is greater. The lap length is multiplied by 1.4 for a tension bar at the top of a section as cast where the cover is less than 2φ, by 1.4 for a corner tension bar where the cover to either face is less than 2φ or the clear distance between adjacent laps is less than 75 mm or 6φ (whichever is greater), and by 2.0 if both conditions apply simultaneously. Splices in tension members must be enclosed in spirals of bar diameter not less than 6 mm at a pitch not exceeding 100 mm. (d) The compression lap length equals the development length in compression but must not be less than 24φ. (e) When two different bar diameters are spliced, the lap is computed using the smaller bar's diameter. (f) For welded wire fabric, the overlap measured between extreme cross wires must be at least the cross-wire spacing plus 100 mm. (g) Bundled bars are spliced one bar at a time and these individual splices must themselves be staggered. Where mechanical splice couplers are used, they shall conform to IS 16172.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Lap = max(L_d, 30φ) flexural tension; max(2·L_d, 30φ) direct tension; max(L_d-comp, 24φ) compression. Straight length ≥ max(15φ, 200 mm). Modifiers: ×1.4 for top bar (cover<2φ), ×1.4 for corner bar (cover<2φ or clear<max(75 mm,6φ)), ×2.0 if both. Different diameters → use smaller. Bars >32 mm: weld (12.4) or mechanically splice (couplers per IS 16172); bundled bars: stagger one at a time; tension splice enclosed by 6 mm spiral, pitch ≤100 mm.

variables.description: lap length for bars in flexural tension (output) — at least the greater of L_d and 30·φ
lap length for bars in direct tension (output) — at least the greater of 2·L_d and 30·φ
lap length for bars in compression (output) — at least the greater of the compression development length L_d^{(c)} and 24·φ
tension development length per 26.2.1 (input)
compression development length (input) — generally shorter than L_d in tension
nominal diameter of the lapped bar (input); for laps of unequal diameters use the smaller bar's diameter

clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 5 (July 2019), p. 45, clause 26.2.5.1(g): appends a closing paragraph on reinforcement couplers. Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is: 'Where mechanical splice couplers are used, they shall conform to IS 16172.' The Amendment No. 5 paragraph operationalises the mechanical-splice alternative introduced by Amendment No. 3 by binding it to the IS 16172 product standard.

Amendment No. 3 (August 2007), p. 45, clause 26.2.5.1(a): wholesale substitution of sub-clause (a). Pre-amendment printed reading: 'Lap splices are not permitted for bars larger than 36 mm — these must be welded (see 12.4); if welding is impracticable, lapping of larger bars is allowed but additional spirals must enclose the lap.' Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is: 'Lap splices shall not be used for bars larger than 32 mm; bars larger than 32 mm shall be welded (see 12.4) or mechanically spliced.' The amendment tightens the upper limit from 36 mm to 32 mm, removes the 'lapping of larger bars with additional spirals' exception, and introduces the mechanical-splice alternative.

clauses.title: Strength of welded and mechanical splices
clauses.text: Welded and mechanical splices may be designed at the strengths below only when the strength of the weld has been verified by tests to be at least equal to that of the parent bar. (a) For splices in compression — both welded splices and mechanical connections may be taken at 100 % of the design strength of the joined bars. (b) For splices in tension — welded bars may be designed at 80 % of the design strength, increased to 100 % only if welding is strictly supervised and at any cross-section of the member not more than 20 % of the tensile reinforcement is welded; mechanical connections may be designed at 100 % of the design strength.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Welded/mechanical splice strengths: 100 % in compression. In tension: welded bars 80 % (100 % if strictly supervised AND ≤20 % of tensile steel welded at any section), mechanical connections 100 %. Requires test verification that weld strength matches the parent bar.

clauses.title: End-bearing splices — compression bars only
clauses.text: End-bearing splices, in which load transfers between bars through direct end-to-end contact, may be used only for bars carrying compression. They are not permitted for bars in tension under any circumstances. The detailing requirements are: the bar ends must be cut square (perpendicular to the bar axis), and concentric bearing between the two end faces must be ensured by a suitable proprietary device or sleeve.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: End-bearing splices permitted only for compression bars; ends cut square and concentric bearing maintained by a suitable device.

clauses.title: Spacing of reinforcement — definition of bar diameter for spacing rules
clauses.text: Per 26.3 (intro), this clause defines what 'diameter' means whenever the spacing or cover rules in this code reference it. For a round bar, the diameter is simply the nominal diameter. For non-round bars, deformed bars, or crimped bars, the diameter is taken as the diameter of a circle whose area equals the bar's effective cross-sectional area (the equivalent-area diameter). The same convention extends to bundled bars: where a spacing limit or a cover requirement under 26.4 is expressed in terms of bar diameter, a bundle of bars in contact is treated as a single equivalent bar whose diameter is derived from the total area of the bundle.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: For spacing/cover rules: bar diameter = nominal diameter (round bar), equivalent-area diameter (non-round/deformed/crimped). A bundle is treated as one bar with diameter from the bundle's total equivalent area.

clauses.title: Minimum distance between individual bars
clauses.text: Three rules set the minimum clear spacing between bars. (a) Horizontal spacing between two parallel main bars must be at least the greatest of: the bar diameter (when the two bars have equal diameter), the larger bar diameter (when they differ), or 5 mm more than the nominal maximum size of coarse aggregate. The attached note clarifies that this does not preclude using a larger aggregate elsewhere in the same member; aggregate may be locally reduced around congested reinforcement to satisfy this rule. (b) Greater horizontal spacing should be provided wherever possible. When needle vibrators are used, the horizontal spacing within a group of bars may be reduced to two-thirds of the nominal maximum aggregate size, provided enough space is left between groups for the vibrator to be inserted. (c) Where bars are arranged in two or more rows, the bars must be vertically aligned and the minimum vertical clear distance between rows must be the greatest of 15 mm, two-thirds of the nominal maximum aggregate size, and the maximum bar diameter.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Min horizontal spacing between main bars = max(larger bar dia, nominal max aggregate + 5 mm). With needle vibrators, intra-group horizontal spacing may drop to 2/3 of nominal max aggregate. Min vertical spacing for multi-row arrangements = max(15 mm, 2/3 nominal max aggregate, largest bar dia).

variables.description: minimum horizontal clear distance between parallel main bars (output)
minimum vertical clear distance between rows of bars (output)
diameter of the largest bar (input)
nominal maximum size of coarse aggregate (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Maximum distance between bars in tension — beams and slabs
clauses.text: Unless an explicit crack-width calculation justifies a wider spacing, this clause caps the maximum spacing between tension bars in flexural members exposed to normal internal or external conditions. (a) For beams, the horizontal distance between parallel reinforcement bars (or groups of bars) near the tension face must not exceed the value tabulated in Table 15, which depends on the percentage of moment redistribution carried out in analysis and the characteristic strength of reinforcement. (b) For slabs, two limits apply by bar function: the horizontal spacing of parallel main reinforcement must not exceed three times the effective depth of the solid slab or 300 mm, whichever is smaller; and the horizontal spacing of bars provided against shrinkage and temperature must not exceed five times the effective depth of the solid slab or 300 mm, whichever is smaller.
clauses.formula_latex: \text{slab main steel: } s \leq \min(3d,\; 300\text{ mm}); \qquad \text{slab S\&T steel: } s \leq \min(5d,\; 300\text{ mm})
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Max bar spacing in tension face: beams use Table 15 (function of moment redistribution and f_y); slab main bars ≤ min(3d, 300 mm); slab shrinkage-and-temperature bars ≤ min(5d, 300 mm). May be relaxed only by an explicit crack-width check.

variables.description: horizontal spacing between parallel tension bars (or bar groups) near the tension face (output)
effective depth of the solid slab (input)

clauses.notes: Amendment No. 3 (August 2007), p. 46, clause 26.3.3(b)(2), last line: substitutes '300 mm' for '450 mm' in the shrinkage-and-temperature bar spacing cap for slabs. Pre-amendment printed reading: '...five times the effective depth of the solid slab or 450 mm, whichever is smaller'. Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is '...or 300 mm, whichever is smaller'. The amendment tightens the absolute cap from 450 mm to 300 mm, bringing the S&T-steel rule closer to the 300 mm cap that already applied to slab main reinforcement under (b)(1). This chunk encodes the post-amendment reading in body, formula_latex, and formula_nl_summary; the variables block was authored against the post-amendment 300 mm cap during Pass 1B.

clauses.title: Nominal cover — definition and minimum bar-diameter rule
clauses.text: Per 26.4 (intro), this section governs the nominal cover to reinforcement; this sub-clause defines the term itself. The nominal cover is the design depth of concrete cover provided to all steel reinforcement, including any links and stirrups. It is the dimension explicitly used in design calculations and explicitly indicated on the structural drawings — distinct from the actual as-cast cover, which may differ due to construction tolerances. As a baseline rule applicable everywhere this code uses 'nominal cover', the value provided shall not be less than the diameter of the bar it covers. Sub-clauses 26.4.2 and 26.4.3 give the additional minimum cover values required for durability and fire resistance respectively.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Nominal cover = design cover depth to all steel including links/stirrups, as shown on drawings; must be at least the bar diameter. Durability and fire impose additional minima (26.4.2, 26.4.3).

variables.description: nominal cover to all steel reinforcement, including links and stirrups (output)
diameter of the bar to which cover is being provided (input); baseline floor is c_nom ≥ φ

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Nominal cover for durability — exposure-based minima
clauses.text: Minimum values of nominal cover for normal-weight aggregate concrete, applicable to all reinforcement including links, are set by the exposure condition the member is subjected to. The five exposure categories (mild, moderate, severe, very severe, extreme) are themselves defined in clause 8.2.2 and depend on the surrounding environment — atmospheric, soil, marine, chemical, and so on. The numerical cover requirements for each exposure are tabulated in Table 16. Note that the column-specific minimum (26.4.2.1) and footing-specific minimum (26.4.2.2) override the Table 16 values where they are larger.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Minimum cover for durability is set by exposure category (mild / moderate / severe / very severe / extreme per 8.2.2) and looked up in Table 16. Column and footing minima from 26.4.2.1 and 26.4.2.2 may govern.
clauses.notes: Amendment No. 1 (June 2001), p. 46, clause 26.4.2: substitutes '8.2.2' for '8.2.3' in the cross-reference to the exposure-condition definitions. Pre-amendment printed reading: '...themselves defined in clause 8.2.3'. Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is '...themselves defined in clause 8.2.2'. The amendment realigns the cross-reference to the clause that actually carries the five-category exposure definitions (8.2.2 in the amended Section 8 structure, where 8.2.3 had originally been the placeholder).

clauses.title: Nominal cover to longitudinal bars in columns
clauses.text: For a longitudinal reinforcing bar in a column, the nominal cover must in any case not be less than 40 mm and not less than the diameter of the bar; the larger of the two governs. A single explicit relaxation is permitted: in columns whose minimum cross-section dimension is 200 mm or under and whose longitudinal bars do not exceed 12 mm in diameter, a nominal cover of 25 mm may be used. This 40 mm baseline overrides the lower exposure-based values that Table 16 would otherwise give for mild or moderate exposure.
clauses.formula_latex: c_{nom,\,col} \;\geq\; \max(40\text{ mm},\;\phi); \quad\text{exception: } 25\text{ mm if } b_{\min} \leq 200\text{ mm and } \phi \leq 12\text{ mm}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Column longitudinal-bar cover ≥ max(40 mm, bar diameter). Relaxation to 25 mm only when min column dimension ≤ 200 mm AND all bars ≤ 12 mm.

variables.description: nominal cover to longitudinal reinforcement in a column (output)
diameter of the longitudinal bar (input); cover must also be at least φ
minimum cross-section dimension of the column (input); the 25 mm relaxation applies only when b_min ≤ 200 mm AND φ ≤ 12 mm

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Nominal cover for footings
clauses.text: For footings, the minimum nominal cover to reinforcement shall be 50 mm. This value applies to all reinforcement in the footing, irrespective of bar diameter, and overrides any lower exposure-based value that Table 16 would otherwise produce for the same exposure category.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Minimum cover for footings is 50 mm, governing over Table 16 where the latter would give a lower value.

variables.description: minimum nominal cover to all reinforcement in a footing — at least 50 mm regardless of bar diameter or exposure category (output)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Nominal cover for fire resistance
clauses.text: Minimum values of nominal cover for normal-weight aggregate concrete, applicable to all reinforcement including links, that are required to achieve a specified period of fire resistance are tabulated in Table 16A. The required cover depends on both the member type (beam, slab, rib, column) and the support condition (simply supported vs continuous), and is indexed by the target fire-resistance period in hours. The notes to Table 16A clarify that the tabulated values relate to the minimum member dimensions given in Fig. 1, and that cases below the bold line in the table additionally require the spalling-mitigation measures of clause 21.3.1.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Cover for fire resistance is looked up in Table 16A, indexed by member type, support condition, and target fire-resistance period in hours. Sub-bold-line cases additionally require anti-spalling measures per 21.3.1.

clauses.title: Beams — minimum and maximum tension reinforcement
clauses.text: Per 26.5 (intro) and the 26.5.1 sub-heading 'Beams', this clause sets bounds on the tension reinforcement in a beam. (a) The minimum area of tension reinforcement shall not be less than that given by A_s / (b·d) = 0.85 / f_y, where A_s is the minimum area of tension reinforcement, b is the breadth of the beam (or the breadth of the web for a flanged section), d is the effective depth, and f_y is the characteristic strength of reinforcement in N/mm². The 0.85 / f_y ratio means a beam with Fe 415 steel needs about 0.205 % minimum, and a Fe 500 beam needs about 0.17 %. (b) The maximum area of tension reinforcement shall not exceed 0.04·b·D, where D is the overall depth of the beam. NOTE — Placing and compacting concrete around 4 % reinforcement may be impractical; designs at lower percentages are recommended where the section permits.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Beam tension steel: minimum ratio A_s/(b·d) ≥ 0.85/f_y (≈0.205 % for Fe 415, ≈0.17 % for Fe 500); maximum area ≤ 0.04·b·D. Use web breadth for flanged sections.

variables.description: area of tension reinforcement in the beam (input/output) — must satisfy both the minimum (lower-bound) and the maximum (upper-bound) limits
maximum permissible area of tension reinforcement in the beam — 0.04·b·D
breadth of the beam (or breadth of the web b_w for flanged sections) (input)
effective depth of the beam (input)
overall depth of the beam (input)
characteristic yield strength of the tension reinforcement (input); the 0.85/f_y ratio gives roughly 0.205% for Fe 415 and 0.17% for Fe 500

clauses.notes: Amendment No. 3 (August 2007), p. 47, clause 26.5.1.1(b): adds a NOTE at end of 26.5.1.1. Pre-amendment printed text had no NOTE following the 0.04·b·D maximum-tension-steel cap. Post-amendment reading — encoded here in paraphrased form — observes that placing and compacting concrete around 4 % reinforcement may be impractical and recommends lower percentages where the design permits.

clauses.title: Beams — compression reinforcement
clauses.text: The maximum area of compression reinforcement in a beam shall not exceed 0.04·b·D, where b is the beam breadth and D the overall depth — the same ceiling that applies to tension reinforcement. Compression reinforcement must be enclosed by stirrups so that the bars are restrained against buckling under compressive load. The arrangement of these stirrups — including the rules on which longitudinal bars must be tied, the maximum unsupported lateral length, and the tie diameter and pitch — is specified in clause 26.5.3.2. NOTE — Placing and compacting concrete around 4 % compression reinforcement may be impractical; designs at lower percentages are recommended where the section permits.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Beam compression steel: maximum area ≤ 0.04·b·D. Must be enclosed in stirrups for lateral restraint, with stirrup arrangement per 26.5.3.2.

variables.description: maximum permissible area of compression reinforcement in the beam — 0.04·b·D (same ceiling as the tension steel maximum)
breadth of the beam (input)
overall depth of the beam (input)

clauses.notes: Amendment No. 3 (August 2007), p. 47, clause 26.5.1.2: adds the same NOTE as 26.5.1.1(b) (see hit S1-06) at end of 26.5.1.2, applied to the maximum compression-steel cap. Pre-amendment printed text had no NOTE. Post-amendment reading — encoded here in paraphrased form — observes that placing and compacting concrete around 4 % compression reinforcement may be impractical and recommends lower percentages where the design permits.

clauses.title: Beams — side-face reinforcement for deep webs
clauses.text: Where the depth of the web in a beam exceeds 750 mm, side-face reinforcement must be provided along the two vertical faces of the web in addition to the main flexural steel. The total area of side-face reinforcement (summed across both faces) shall be at least 0.1 % of the web area. The reinforcement must be distributed equally on the two faces, and the spacing between bars on each face shall not exceed 300 mm or the web thickness, whichever is less.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: When web depth > 750 mm in a beam: provide side-face steel ≥ 0.1 % of web area, split equally between two faces, at spacing ≤ min(300 mm, web thickness).

variables.description: total side-face reinforcement area, summed across the two vertical web faces (output) — at least 0.1% of the web area
web breadth of the beam (input); web depth times b_w gives the web area used for the 0.1% rule
web depth (input); when the web depth exceeds 750 mm, side-face reinforcement is required
spacing between side-face bars along each face (output) — capped at min(300 mm, b_w)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Beams — transverse reinforcement enclosure rule
clauses.text: Transverse reinforcement in a beam (the stirrups or links provided for shear and torsion) shall be taken around the outermost tension and compression bars; in other words, the stirrups must enclose the full longitudinal-bar cage rather than only an inner subset of bars. For T-beams and I-beams, this enclosure rule extends into the flange: the transverse reinforcement must additionally pass around any longitudinal bars located close to the outer face of the flange. The rule defines the geometric path of the stirrup and applies regardless of the actual size or spacing of the stirrups, which are governed by clauses 26.5.1.5 and 26.5.1.6.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Beam stirrups must enclose the outermost tension AND compression longitudinal bars. In T- and I-beams, stirrups must also wrap around longitudinal bars near the outer face of the flange.

clauses.title: Beams — maximum spacing of shear reinforcement
clauses.text: The maximum spacing of shear reinforcement, measured along the axis of the member, is capped by two limits. For vertical stirrups, the spacing must not exceed 0.75·d, where d is the effective depth of the section under consideration. For inclined stirrups at 45 degrees, the spacing must not exceed d. In addition, in no case shall the spacing of shear reinforcement exceed 300 mm. Both the angle-dependent limit and the absolute 300 mm cap are checked, and the smaller governs.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Beam shear reinforcement spacing ≤ 0.75d (vertical stirrups) or d (inclined stirrups at 45°); in either case ≤ 300 mm absolute. d = effective depth.

variables.description: spacing of shear reinforcement (stirrups) measured along the member axis (output); the smaller of the angle-dependent limit and the absolute 300 mm cap governs
effective depth of the beam (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Beams — minimum shear reinforcement
clauses.text: Minimum shear reinforcement, in the form of stirrups, must be provided in beams such that A_sv / (b · s_v) ≥ 0.4 / (0.87 · f_y), where A_sv is the total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs effective in shear, s_v is the stirrup spacing along the length of the member, b is the breadth of the beam (or the breadth of the web for a flanged beam), and f_y is the characteristic strength of the stirrup reinforcement in N/mm² and shall not be taken greater than 415 N/mm² when applying this formula. The minimum-shear-reinforcement requirement may be waived only when both of the following hold: the maximum shear stress calculated for the section is less than half the permissible value, AND the member is of minor structural importance, such as a lintel.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Minimum shear reinforcement: A_sv/(b·s_v) ≥ 0.4/(0.87·f_y), with f_y capped at 415 N/mm² for this check. Waiver only if max shear stress < 0.5 × permissible AND member is of minor structural importance (e.g. lintel).

variables.description: total cross-sectional area of stirrup legs effective in shear at the section (input)
breadth of the beam (or breadth of the web b_w for flanged sections) (input)
spacing of the stirrups along the member length (input)
characteristic yield strength of the stirrup reinforcement (input) — capped at 415 N/mm² when applying this formula

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Beams — distribution of torsion reinforcement
clauses.text: When a member is designed for torsion (per Section 41 for limit state design or Annex B-6 for working stress design), torsion reinforcement must be detailed as follows. (a) The transverse reinforcement for torsion must be in the form of rectangular closed stirrups placed perpendicular to the member axis; the spacing of these closed stirrups must not exceed the least of three values: x_1 (the short dimension of the stirrup), (x_1 + y_1)/4 (where y_1 is the long dimension), and 300 mm. (b) Longitudinal reinforcement must be placed as close to the corners of the cross-section as practicable, with at least one longitudinal bar in each corner of the closed ties. Where the cross-sectional dimension of the member exceeds 450 mm, additional longitudinal bars must be provided to satisfy the minimum reinforcement and spacing requirements of 26.5.1.3 (the side-face reinforcement rule applied here for torsion).
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Torsion reinforcement: rectangular closed stirrups perpendicular to member axis, spacing ≤ min(x_1, (x_1+y_1)/4, 300 mm) where x_1 < y_1 are the stirrup short and long dimensions. Longitudinal bar in each corner; for member dimensions > 450 mm, add longitudinal bars per 26.5.1.3.

variables.description: spacing of closed stirrups for torsion (output) — the least of x_1, (x_1 + y_1)/4, and 300 mm governs
short dimension of the rectangular closed stirrup (centre-to-centre of corner bars in the narrow direction) (input)
long dimension of the rectangular closed stirrup (centre-to-centre of corner bars in the long direction) (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Beams — reinforcement in flanges of T- and L-beams
clauses.text: Reinforcement in the flanges of T-beams and L-beams must satisfy the requirements of clause 23.1.1(b). When the flanges are in tension (e.g. over a continuous-beam support), a part of the main tension reinforcement must be distributed across either the effective flange width or a width equal to one-tenth of the span, whichever is the smaller. If the effective flange width exceeds one-tenth of the span, nominal longitudinal reinforcement must additionally be provided in the outer portions of the flange — that is, in the part of the flange that lies beyond the span/10 zone of distributed main tension steel.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: T- and L-beam flanges: per 23.1.1(b). Where flange in tension, distribute part of main tension steel over min(effective flange width, span/10). If effective flange width > span/10, provide nominal longitudinal steel in the outer flange portions beyond the span/10 zone.

clauses.title: Slabs — minimum reinforcement and maximum bar diameter
clauses.text: Per 26.5.2 (intro), the rules below apply to slabs in addition to any other applicable requirements. Per 26.5.2.1 (Minimum reinforcement): the area of mild-steel reinforcement in either direction in a slab shall not be less than 0.15 percent of the total cross-sectional area of the slab. This baseline may be reduced to 0.12 percent when high-strength deformed bars or welded-wire fabric is used in place of mild steel. Per 26.5.2.2 (Maximum diameter): the diameter of any reinforcing bar in a slab shall not exceed one-eighth of the total thickness of the slab.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Slab minimum reinforcement: 0.15 % of gross area for mild steel, 0.12 % for HSD bars or welded wire fabric. Maximum bar diameter ≤ slab thickness / 8.

variables.description: minimum area of distribution reinforcement in either direction in a slab (output)
total cross-sectional area of the slab (per metre run × slab thickness) (input)
maximum permissible bar diameter in a slab (output) — capped at one-eighth of the total slab thickness
total thickness of the slab (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Columns — longitudinal reinforcement (general, helical, and pedestal rules)
clauses.text: Per 26.5.3 (intro), this clause governs longitudinal steel in columns and contains eight sub-rules. (a) The cross-sectional area of longitudinal reinforcement shall be not less than 0.8 percent and not more than 6 percent of the gross cross-sectional area of the column; the attached note observes that 6 percent causes practical placing and compaction difficulties, so lower percentages are recommended, and where bars from the column below are lapped with those above, the percentage usually should not exceed 4 percent. (b) For a column whose cross-section is larger than required by load, the minimum percentage may be computed against the area of concrete actually required to resist the direct stress, not against the actual gross area. (c) The minimum number of longitudinal bars is four for rectangular columns and six for circular columns. (d) Bars shall be not less than 12 mm in diameter. (e) A column reinforced with helical transverse reinforcement shall have at least six longitudinal bars within the helical. (f) In a helically reinforced column, the longitudinal bars shall be in contact with the helical and equidistant around its inner circumference. (g) The spacing of longitudinal bars measured along the periphery of the column shall not exceed 300 mm. (h) For pedestals (compression members whose effective length does not exceed three times the least lateral dimension) in which longitudinal reinforcement is not used in strength calculations, nominal longitudinal reinforcement of not less than 0.15 percent of the cross-sectional area shall still be provided.
clauses.formula_latex: 0.008\,A_{gross} \;\leq\; A_{sc} \;\leq\; 0.06\,A_{gross} \;\;(\leq 0.04 \text{ recommended at lap}); \quad N_{\min,\,bars} = 4\,(\text{rect}),\;6\,(\text{circ},\;\text{helical}); \quad \phi \geq 12\text{ mm}; \quad s_{periphery} \leq 300\text{ mm}; \quad A_{sc,\,pedestal} \geq 0.0015\,A_{gross}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Column longitudinal steel: 0.8 % to 6 % of gross area (4 % recommended at laps). Minimum 4 bars (rectangular) or 6 bars (circular, also helical). Min bar diameter 12 mm. Helical columns: longitudinal bars in contact with helix, equidistant around inner circumference. Peripheral spacing ≤ 300 mm. Pedestals (effective length ≤ 3× least lateral dim) without longitudinal in calc still need ≥ 0.15 % nominal.

variables.description: cross-sectional area of longitudinal reinforcement in the column (input/output) — bounded between 0.8% and 6% of A_gross
gross cross-sectional area of the column (input)
minimum number of longitudinal bars in the column (output) — 4 for rectangular columns, 6 for circular (helically reinforced) columns
diameter of a longitudinal bar (input); shall be ≥ 12 mm

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Columns — lateral ties: general, arrangement, pitch, and diameter
clauses.text: This clause sets out transverse reinforcement using polygonal lateral ties (see clause cl_26_5_3_2_helicals for the helical option). (a) General: a reinforced concrete compression member shall have transverse reinforcement so disposed that every longitudinal bar nearest to the compression face has effective lateral support against buckling. The lateral support is provided by transverse reinforcement either in the form of circular rings (taking up circumferential tension) or polygonal links/lateral ties whose internal angles do not exceed 135°; ends of the transverse reinforcement must be properly anchored per 26.2.2.4(b). (b) Arrangement: (1) if longitudinal bars are not spaced more than 75 mm on either side, transverse reinforcement need only loop around corner and alternate bars (see Fig. 8); (2) if longitudinal bars are spaced ≤ 48 times the tie diameter and are effectively tied in two directions, additional intermediate bars need be tied in one direction only by open ties (see Fig. 9); (3) where longitudinal bars sit in more than one row, lateral support to inner-row bars may be assumed provided the outer-most row is tied per 26.5.3.2 AND no inner-row bar is closer than three times the inner-row largest bar diameter to the nearest compression face (see Fig. 10); (4) where longitudinal bars are grouped (not in contact) and each group is adequately tied, the transverse reinforcement for the column as a whole may treat each group as a single bar for pitch and diameter calculations, and the diameter of the transverse reinforcement need not exceed 20 mm in this case (see Fig. 11). (c) Pitch and diameter: the pitch of transverse reinforcement shall not exceed the least of the column's least lateral dimension, 16 times the smallest tied-longitudinal-bar diameter, and 300 mm. The tie diameter shall be at least one-fourth of the diameter of the largest longitudinal bar, and in no case less than 6 mm.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Column lateral ties cover three rules. General: every longitudinal bar near compression face has lateral support via rings or polygonal links (≤135° angles). Arrangement: 75 mm rule, 48φ_tie rule, multi-row rule, grouped-bars rule (group = single bar; tie ≤ 20 mm). Pitch ≤ min(least lateral dim, 16·smallest long bar dia, 300 mm). Tie diameter ≥ max(φ_long_max / 4, 6 mm) per Am-1.

variables.description: spacing of lateral ties along the column axis (output) — the least of the column's least lateral dimension b_min, 16·φ_{long,min}, and 300 mm governs
diameter of the lateral tie (output) — at least one-fourth of the largest longitudinal bar diameter and in no case less than 6 mm (post-Am-1)
least lateral dimension of the column cross-section (input)
smallest tied-longitudinal-bar diameter in the column (input)
largest longitudinal-bar diameter in the column (input)

clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 1 (June 2001), p. 49, clause 26.5.3.2(c)(2), last line: substitutes '6 mm' for '16 mm' in the lower-bound tie-diameter requirement. Pre-amendment printed reading: 'in no case less than 16 mm' — dimensionally inconsistent with the upstream rule that the tie diameter need only be one-quarter of the largest longitudinal bar diameter, since a 6 mm floor is the engineering-practice value (16 mm would mandate a tie larger than most longitudinal bars). Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is 'in no case less than 6 mm'. The 2021 BIS reprint of IS 456:2000 incorporates this Amendment No. 1 correction. This chunk encodes the post-amendment reading directly in body, formula_latex, and formula_nl_summary; the variables block was authored against the post-amendment 6 mm floor during Pass 1B.

clauses.title: Columns — helical reinforcement: pitch and diameter
clauses.text: This sub-clause is sub-point (d) of 26.5.3.2 and covers helical transverse reinforcement (the alternative to polygonal lateral ties from cl_26_5_3_2_lateral_ties). (1) Pitch: helical reinforcement shall be of regular formation with the turns of the helix spaced evenly, and its ends shall be anchored properly by providing one and a half extra turns of the spiral bar. Where the column design relies on the strength contribution of the helical reinforcement (i.e. the increased axial load capacity allowed when helicals confine the core), the pitch of the helical turns must lie within all four of the following bounds: not more than 75 mm, not more than one-sixth of the core diameter of the column, not less than 25 mm, and not less than three times the diameter of the steel bar forming the helix. In any other case (where the design does not rely on the helical strength contribution), the general column transverse-reinforcement requirements of 26.5.3.2 (i.e. the lateral-tie pitch and diameter rules) must be complied with instead. (2) Diameter: the diameter of the helical reinforcement is governed by the same rule as for polygonal links — see clause 26.5.3.2 (c)(2), captured in cl_26_5_3_2_lateral_ties.
clauses.formula_latex: p_{helix} \in \left[\max(25\text{ mm},\; 3\phi_{helix}),\; \min(75\text{ mm},\; D_{core}/6)\right] \;\;\text{(when relying on helical strength)}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Helical reinforcement: regular formation, ends anchored with 1.5 extra turns. When the design uses the helical strength enhancement: pitch must be ≤ 75 mm AND ≤ core_dia/6 AND ≥ 25 mm AND ≥ 3·helical_bar_dia. Otherwise, fall back to the lateral-tie rules of 26.5.3.2. Helix diameter follows the same rule as lateral ties (φ_long_max / 4 floor, with PDF's 16 mm absolute floor flagged for verification).

variables.description: pitch of the helical reinforcement (output) — when the column design relies on the helical strength contribution, must lie in [max(25, 3·φ_helix), min(75, D_core/6)]
diameter of the steel bar forming the helix (input)
core diameter of the column, measured to the outside of the helix (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Columns — offset bars at splices
clauses.text: Where longitudinal bars are offset (cranked) at a splice — typically because the column above is smaller than the column below — the geometry and detailing must satisfy several rules. The slope of the inclined portion of the bar with respect to the column axis must not exceed 1 in 6, and the portions of the bar above and below the offset must run parallel to the column axis. Adequate horizontal support must be provided at the offset bends, treated as a matter of design and supplied by metal ties, spirals, or parts of the floor construction; these supports must be placed within a distance of not more than eight bar diameters from the point of bend. The horizontal thrust to be resisted at each bend shall be taken as one and a half times the horizontal component of the nominal stress in the inclined portion of the bar. Offset bars must be bent before they are placed in the forms, not after. Where the column faces are offset by 75 mm or more, the inclined-bar method is not permitted: instead, splices for the vertical bars adjacent to the offset face must be made via separate dowels overlapped per 26.2.5.1.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Column offset bars: slope ≤ 1 in 6; portions above and below parallel to column axis. Horizontal support (ties, spirals, or floor) within 8φ of the bend, designed for 1.5× the horizontal component of nominal bar stress in the inclined portion. Bars bent before placing in forms. If face offset ≥ 75 mm, use separate dowels overlapped per 26.2.5.1 instead.

variables.description: slope of the inclined (cranked) portion of the bar with respect to the column axis (output) — must not exceed 1 in 6
distance from the point of bend to the nearest horizontal support (tie, spiral, or floor component) (output) — must be ≤ 8·φ
diameter of the offset bar (input)
horizontal thrust to be resisted at each bend (output) — 1.5 times the horizontal component of the nominal stress in the inclined bar portion
horizontal component of the nominal stress (force) in the inclined portion of the bar (input)

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Assumptions for the limit state of collapse in flexure
clauses.text: Design for the limit state of collapse in flexure rests on six assumptions taken together. (a) Plane sections normal to the member axis remain plane after bending — strain therefore varies linearly through the depth. (b) The maximum compressive strain in concrete at the outermost compression fibre is 0.0035 in bending. (c) The relationship between compressive stress and strain in concrete may be modelled as a rectangle, trapezoid, parabola, or any other shape that gives strength predictions in substantial agreement with test results; the parabolic-rectangular curve of Fig 21 is one acceptable choice. The design compressive strength is taken as 0.67·fck and the partial safety factor γm = 1.5 is applied in addition. The NOTE under 38.1(c) summarises the resulting design stress block (Fig 22): the resultant compressive force is 0.36·fck·xu·b and acts at 0.42·xu measured from the extreme compression fibre, where xu is the depth of the neutral axis. (d) The tensile strength of concrete is ignored. (e) Stresses in the reinforcement are derived from the steel's representative stress-strain curve — Fig 23 gives typical curves for cold-worked deformed bars (23A) and for steel with a definite yield point (23B); the partial safety factor γm = 1.15 applies. (f) The strain in the tension reinforcement at the section at failure must not be less than fy/(1.15·Es) + 0.002. This lower bound on tensile strain is what makes the section under-reinforced (steel-controlled) and ensures the steel yields before the concrete crushes. The limiting depths of neutral axis xu,max/d implied by these assumptions for fy = 250, 415, 500 N/mm² are tabulated in the trailing NOTE (encoded as a separate synthetic table). Moment-of-resistance expressions for rectangular and T-sections derived from these assumptions are given in Annex G of the code.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Six assumptions for flexural limit-state design: plane sections, εcu = 0.0035 in bending, parabolic-rectangular concrete with peak design stress 0.67·fck/γm and γm = 1.5 (giving stress-block resultant 0.36·fck·xu at lever arm 0.42·xu), zero concrete tensile strength, steel stress from its σ-ε curve with γm = 1.15, and minimum tension-steel strain at failure of fy/(1.15·Es) + 0.002.

variables.description: lower bound on the strain in the tension reinforcement at the section at failure (output) — ensures the section is under-reinforced (steel-controlled) so steel yields before concrete crushes
characteristic yield strength of the tension reinforcement (input)
modulus of elasticity of steel reinforcement (input) — typically taken as 2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²

clauses.notes: Variables block backfilled during Phase 1B rectification.

clauses.title: Stress-strain curve for concrete (limit state design)
clauses.caption: Parabolic-rectangular stress-strain relationship for concrete in flexural compression. Two curves are shown on the same axes: an upper characteristic curve peaking at 0.67·fck, and a lower design curve peaking at 0.67·fck/γm with γm = 1.5 (so the design peak is 0.67·fck/1.5 = 0.446·fck). Both curves rise parabolically from the origin to their respective peaks at strain 0.002, then sit on a horizontal plateau out to the ultimate compressive strain 0.0035 specified by 38.1(b). Integrating the design curve over the compression zone of depth xu yields the rectangular stress block of Fig 22 — resultant force 0.36·fck·xu·b acting at 0.42·xu from the extreme compression fibre — as stated in the NOTE under 38.1(c).

segments.description: Parabolic rise from origin to peak 0.67·fck at strain 0.002.
Constant plateau at 0.67·fck from strain 0.002 to ultimate strain 0.0035.
Parabolic rise from origin to design peak 0.67·fck/γm = 0.446·fck at strain 0.002 (with γm = 1.5).
Constant plateau at 0.67·fck/γm from strain 0.002 to ultimate strain 0.0035.

stress_block_consequences.note: 0.36 and 0.42 are integration factors of the design curve over a compression zone of depth xu, with linear strain distribution reaching 0.0035 at the extreme fibre. They are the standard IS 456 stress-block parameters quoted in the NOTE under 38.1(c) and labelled in Fig 22.

clauses.nl_caption: Closed-form parabolic-rectangular concrete stress-strain curve used in flexural limit-state design. The design curve rises parabolically from the origin to peak 0.67·fck/γm (with γm = 1.5) at strain 0.002, then stays constant up to the ultimate strain 0.0035. Integrating over the compression zone gives the standard IS 456 rectangular stress-block resultant 0.36·fck·xu acting at 0.42·xu from the extreme compression fibre.

clauses.title: Expansion joints — required where marked changes in plan dimensions occur abruptly; joint design rules
clauses.text:
Structures in which marked changes in plan dimensions take place abruptly shall be provided with expansion joints at the section where such changes occur. Expansion joints shall be so provided that the necessary movement occurs with a minimum resistance at the joint. The structures adjacent to the joint should preferably be supported on separate columns or walls but not necessarily on separate foundations. Reinforcement shall not extend across an expansion joint and the break between the sections shall be complete.

The rule comprises three operational requirements. First — joints must be placed where abrupt plan-dimension changes occur (re-entrant corners, large set-backs, junctions of differently-loaded blocks) to relieve the differential stress concentration that would otherwise develop. Second — the joint must permit movement with low resistance; tight or interlocked joints that frictionally resist movement defeat the purpose. Third — the structural separation must be complete: reinforcement passing across the joint would tie the segments together and again defeat the purpose. Separate foundations are preferred but not mandated — the joint itself need only be above the foundation level, allowing a continuous mat under the joint where soil conditions favour it.
clauses.notes: Pairs operationally with clause 19.5.1 (small-building exemption at 45 m lateral dimension) and clause 27.2 (length-triggered expansion joint provisions). The 'reinforcement shall not extend across' rule is the operational distinction between an expansion joint (complete break) and a construction joint or contraction joint (which allow continuity).

clauses.title: Expansion joints — length-triggered provision at 45 m; details left to designer with reference to IS 3414
clauses.text:
The details as to the length of a structure where expansion joints have to be provided can be determined after taking into consideration various factors, such as temperature, exposure to weather, the time and season of the laying of the concrete, and other factors. Normally structures exceeding 45 metres in length are designed with one or more expansion joints. However, in view of the large number of factors involved in deciding the location, spacing, and nature of expansion joints, the provision of expansion joint in reinforced cement concrete structures should be left to the discretion of the designer. IS 3414 gives the design considerations, which need to be examined and provided for.

The 45-metre trigger is operationally important: it is the same threshold at which the small-building shrinkage/creep/temperature exemption of clause 19.5.1 stops applying. For lengths up to 45 metres, no expansion joint is normally needed and the temperature/shrinkage/creep effects can be ignored; beyond 45 metres, the structure must accommodate the differential strains either by expansion joints or by explicit analysis with full accounting of the named effects. The clause is non-prescriptive on details — there are no formulas for joint width, no rules for joint frequency beyond the 45-metre trigger — recognising the multitude of factors involved.
clauses.notes: Closing clause of Section 27. References IS 3414 (Code of Practice for Design of Joints in Buildings — outside the IS 456 corpus). The 45-metre threshold matches clause 19.5.1.

clauses.title: Concrete corbel — defined as short cantilever where a_v ≤ d and outer-edge depth ≥ half the root depth; depth at face per clause 40.5.1
clauses.text:
A corbel is a short cantilever projection which supports a load-bearing member and where:

(a) the distance a_v between the line of the reaction to the supported load and the root of the corbel is less than d (the effective depth of the root of the corbel); and

(b) the depth at the outer edge of the contact area is not less than one-half of the depth at the root of the corbel.

The depth of the corbel at the face of the support is determined in accordance with clause 40.5.1.

The two-part definition isolates the corbel from ordinary short cantilevers by enforcing the short-shear-span condition (a_v < d) and the trapezoidal/tapered profile (outer-edge depth at least half the root depth). Under these conditions the load-transfer mechanism is strut-and-tie rather than ordinary flexure-shear: a compressive strut runs diagonally from the load point down to the column face, balanced by a horizontal tie at the top of the corbel and the column reaction at the base. Ordinary beam-design provisions of Section 22 do not apply; the corbel-specific provisions of clauses 28.2.1 to 28.2.4 (here) and the depth-at-face shear check of clause 40.5.1 (Session 2) do.
clauses.notes: The a_v < d short-shear-span condition is the geometric trigger for strut-and-tie behaviour. The half-depth taper condition restricts the geometry to genuinely trapezoidal/tapered corbels (excluding deep rectangular brackets). Forward reference to clause 40.5.1 (Section 40 LSM shear, Session 2) — that is where the depth-at-face shear check lives.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: A short cantilever is classified as a corbel only if BOTH conditions hold: (1) the shear span a_v from the line of reaction to the corbel root is strictly less than the effective depth d at the root; AND (2) the depth at the outer edge of the contact area is at least half the depth at the root. Failing either, the projection is treated as an ordinary cantilever.

variables.description: shear span — horizontal distance from line of reaction of supported load to root of corbel (input)
effective depth at the root of the corbel (input)
depth at the outer edge of the contact area of the supported load (input)
depth at the root of the corbel (input — same as the full-depth dimension at the column face)

clauses.title: Corbel design — header introducing 28.2.1 to 28.2.4
clauses.text: This clause opens the design subsection of corbel rules. Four sub-clauses follow: 28.2.1 simplifying-assumptions (strut-and-tie idealisation with two guideline rules), 28.2.2 reinforcement anchorage (front-face anchorage requirement), 28.2.3 shear reinforcement (horizontal links in upper two-thirds of effective depth), and 28.2.4 resistance to applied horizontal force (additional reinforcement connected to the supported member).
clauses.notes: Header clause for Section 28.2. Authored separately to preserve the design-subsection grouping.

clauses.title: Corbel simplifying assumptions — strut-and-tie idealisation with (a) horizontal-force-share ≥ 50 % of design vertical load and (b) strain compatibility at root
clauses.text:
The concrete and reinforcement of a corbel may be assumed to act as elements of a simple strut-and-tie system, subject to the following guidelines:

(a) The magnitude of the resistance provided to horizontal force should be not less than one-half of the design vertical load on the corbel (see also clause 28.2.4).

(b) Compatibility of strains between the strut-and-tie members at the corbel root should be ensured.

It should be noted that the horizontal link requirement described in clause 28.2.3 will ensure satisfactory serviceability performance.

The two assumptions narrow the strut-and-tie model from a generic geometric construction to a specific load case. Assumption (a) is a minimum-horizontal-tie-force rule: even when no externally applied horizontal force is named, the design tie at the top of the corbel must resist at least 50 percent of the vertical load applied to the corbel. This accounts for unintended eccentricity, friction effects from the bearing surface, and overall robustness. Assumption (b) is an internal-consistency rule: the strain in the tie at the corbel root must match the strain in the compression strut, ensuring the model is kinematically valid. The closing observation directs designers to clause 28.2.3 for the horizontal-link prescription that delivers serviceability.
clauses.notes: The 50-percent horizontal-tie rule is a robustness floor — even an apparently purely-vertical-loaded corbel must be designed for some horizontal action. The cross-reference to 28.2.4 makes the link to the explicit horizontal-force case.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Under the strut-and-tie idealisation, the horizontal-force resistance at the corbel face must be at least one-half of the design vertical load on the corbel.

variables.description: horizontal-force resistance provided at the front face of the corbel (output — tie capacity)
design vertical load on the corbel (input)
