clauses.title: Corbel reinforcement anchorage — at front face of corbel, either by welded transverse bar (equal strength) or by bending bars back into a loop
clauses.text:
At the front face of the corbel, the reinforcement should be anchored by one of the following methods:

(a) welding to a transverse bar of equal strength — in this case the bearing area of the load should stop short of the face of the support by a distance equal to the cover of the tie reinforcement; or

(b) bending back the bars to form a loop — in this case the bearing area of the load should not project beyond the straight portion of the bars forming the main tension reinforcement.

Both routes ensure that the tie reinforcement is anchored beyond the point of load application — without anchorage at the front face the tie would yield prematurely with progressive slip. Route (a) uses a welded crossbar of equal cross-sectional strength as the anchorage device; the geometric constraint that the bearing area must stop short of the support face by the cover distance is to prevent the load from being applied directly over the unanchored portion of the bar. Route (b) achieves the same anchorage by bending the bar back into a U-loop; the geometric constraint here is that the load may not be applied beyond the straight portion of the main tension bar.
clauses.notes: Two anchorage routes. Route (a) welded crossbar requires high-quality weld inspection; route (b) bent-back loop is the more common practical choice. Both have explicit geometric constraints on bearing-area location relative to the anchorage.

clauses.title: LSM — Characteristic strength of materials: 5 % fractile (concrete per Table 2; steel = min yield stress / 0.2 % proof stress)
clauses.text: The term 'characteristic strength' means that value of the strength of the material below which not more than 5 percent of the test results are expected to fall. The characteristic strength for concrete shall be in accordance with Table 2. Until the relevant Indian Standard Specifications for reinforcing steel are modified to include the concept of characteristic strength, the characteristic value shall be assumed as the minimum yield stress / 0.2 percent proof stress specified in the relevant Indian Standard Specifications.
clauses.formula_latex: P(\sigma_{test} < f_{ck}) = 0.05 \quad \text{(5\% fractile definition)} \\ \text{Concrete: } f_{ck} \text{ per Table 2} \\ \text{Steel: } f_y = \min(\text{yield stress}, \, 0.2\% \text{ proof stress})
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Characteristic strength is the 5% fractile of the strength distribution — i.e. the value below which at most 5% of test results fall. For concrete, the characteristic compressive strength f_ck is given by Table 2 (Session 4). For reinforcing steel, in the absence of explicit characteristic-strength specifications in IS standards, use the minimum yield stress (or 0.2% proof stress) from the relevant IS specification as the characteristic value.

variables.description: characteristic compressive strength of concrete (5% fractile) (output)
characteristic strength of reinforcing steel = min yield / 0.2% proof stress (output)

clauses.notes:
Foundational definition for LSM. The 5% fractile is the statistical convention — 95% of test results lie at or above the characteristic value, which means the design value (after γ_m reduction) covers the realistic worst case.

For concrete, Table 2 (Session 4) lists f_ck for each grade (M-15, M-20, M-25, ... M-80) — these are the characteristic 28-day cube strengths.

For steel, the IS standards (e.g. IS 1786 for HYSD bars) typically give a 'minimum yield stress' or '0.2% proof stress' which is used as the characteristic value pending update to explicit fractile-based specifications.

clauses.title: LSM — Characteristic loads: 95 % non-exceedance probability; per IS 875 (DL/IL/WL/SL) and IS 1893 (seismic)
clauses.text: The term 'characteristic load' means that value of load which has a 95 percent probability of not being exceeded during the life of the structure. Since data are not available to express loads in statistical terms, for the purpose of this standard, dead loads given in IS 875 (Part 1), imposed loads given in IS 875 (Part 2), wind loads given in IS 875 (Part 3), snow load as given in IS 875 (Part 4) and seismic forces given in IS 1893 shall be assumed as the characteristic loads.
clauses.formula_latex: P(F_{life} > F_{characteristic}) \leq 0.05 \quad \text{(95\% non-exceedance)} \\ \text{Dead load: IS 875 (Part 1)} \\ \text{Imposed load: IS 875 (Part 2)} \\ \text{Wind load: IS 875 (Part 3)} \\ \text{Snow load: IS 875 (Part 4)} \\ \text{Seismic forces: IS 1893}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Characteristic load is the load value with 95% probability of not being exceeded during the structure's design life. Because statistical load data are not generally available, IS 456 defers to external standards: IS 875 Parts 1-4 (dead, imposed, wind, snow) and IS 1893 (seismic). Values from these standards are taken as characteristic loads.

variables.description: characteristic load (95% non-exceedance) (output)
actual load realised over the structure's life (statistical quantity)

clauses.notes:
Foundational definition. The 95% non-exceedance probability is the conventional load-side counterpart to the 5% fractile for material strength. Note this clause defers entirely to IS 875 (Loads) and IS 1893 (Seismic) for the actual numerical values — IS 456 only assigns them the LABEL 'characteristic'.

For compliance checking, the vetting layer must obtain DL/IL/WL/SL/EL values from IS 875 / IS 1893 and apply Table 18 γ_f factors per 36.4.1 to obtain design values.

clauses.title: LSM — Design Values: section header
clauses.text: Section 36.3 defines the design values for materials (36.3.1 — f_d = f/γ_m) and loads (36.3.2 — F_d = F·γ_f), and the consequences-of-failure adjustment (36.3.3 — higher γ values for high-consequence structures).
clauses.notes: Header chunk anchoring 36.3.1, 36.3.2, 36.3.3.

clauses.title: LSM — Design strength of materials: f_d = f / γ_m
clauses.text:
The design strength of the materials, f_d, is given by:

f_d = f / γ_m

where f = characteristic strength of the material (see 36.1), and γ_m = partial safety factor appropriate to the material and the limit state being considered.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Design strength f_d equals the characteristic strength f divided by γ_m, the partial safety factor for the material and the limit state in question. For concrete in limit-state-of-collapse, γ_m = 1.5; for steel, γ_m = 1.15 (per 36.4.2.1). For serviceability (deflection check) the material properties use characteristic strength directly (per 36.4.2.2 — i.e. γ_m = 1.0 effectively).

variables.description: design strength of the material (output)
characteristic strength of the material per 36.1 (input)
partial safety factor for material strength per 36.4.2 (input)

clauses.notes: Closes many forward references from prior sessions to 'γ_m'. The formula is the LSM-core material-strength reduction. For typical M-25 concrete in collapse: f_cd = 25/1.5 = 16.67 N/mm². For Fe415 steel in collapse: f_yd = 415/1.15 = 360.87 N/mm². For deflection (serviceability), γ_m = 1.0 effectively per 36.4.2.2.

clauses.title: LSM — Design load: F_d = F · γ_f
clauses.text:
The design load, F_d, is given by:

F_d = F · γ_f

where F = characteristic load (see 36.2), and γ_f = partial safety factor appropriate to the nature of loading and the limit state being considered.
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Design load F_d equals the characteristic load F multiplied by γ_f, the partial safety factor for the load type and limit state. γ_f values come from Table 18 (per 36.4.1) and depend on which loads are combined (DL+IL, DL+WL, DL+IL+WL) and which limit state is checked (Collapse or Serviceability).

variables.description: design load (output)
characteristic load per 36.2 (input)
partial safety factor for loads per Table 18 (input)

clauses.notes: Closes many forward references from prior sessions to 'γ_f' and 'Table 18'. The formula is the LSM-core load amplification. For DL+IL collapse: F_d,DL = 1.5·F_DL, F_d,IL = 1.5·F_IL. For DL+WL collapse: F_d,DL = 1.5·F_DL, F_d,WL = 1.5·F_WL (or DL factor 0.9 if WL governs stability per Table 18 footnote 1). For serviceability: typically γ_f = 1.0 across the board.

clauses.title: LSM — Consequences of attaining limit state: higher γ_f and γ_m permitted for high-consequence structures
clauses.text: Where the consequences of a structure attaining a limit state are of a serious nature such as huge loss of life and disruption of the economy, higher values for γ_f and γ_m than those given under 36.4.1 and 36.4.2 may be applied.
clauses.notes: Permissive clause allowing the designer to use HIGHER (more conservative) values than the standard Table 18 / 36.4.2 factors for critical structures. Examples (not in code but typical): nuclear containment, defence structures, evacuation routes, hospitals' critical care wings. The clause does not specify HOW MUCH higher — left to designer / project-specific specification.

clauses.title: LSM — Partial Safety Factors: section header
clauses.text: Section 36.4 defines the two families of partial safety factors: 36.4.1 — γ_f for loads (per Table 18); 36.4.2 — γ_m for material strength (1.5 for concrete, 1.15 for steel, per 36.4.2.1; for deflection use characteristic strength per 36.4.2.2).
clauses.notes: Header chunk anchoring 36.4.1 and 36.4.2.

clauses.title: LSM — Partial safety factor γ_f for loads: values per Table 18
clauses.text: The values of γ_f given in Table 18 shall normally be used.
clauses.notes:
Single-sentence pointer clause. The substantive content lives in Table 18 (this batch). The phrase 'shall normally be used' allows for the 36.3.3 high-consequence-structure exception.

This clause is heavily forward-referenced from many prior-session chunks (anywhere γ_f or Table 18 is mentioned).

clauses.title: Table 18 — Values of partial safety factor γ_f for loads (referenced by clauses 18.2.3.1, 36.4.1, B-4.3) — Am-1 substitutes '−' for '1.0' in col 4 against DL+IL
clauses.caption: Partial safety factor γ_f values for three standard load combinations (DL+IL, DL+WL, DL+IL+WL) under two limit states (Collapse and Serviceability), each split into three load types (DL, IL, WL). Six γ_f columns plus the load-combination row label. Notes: (1) for earthquake effects, substitute EL for WL; (2) serviceability values are short-term; for long-term creep, only DL and the permanent fraction of IL are considered; (¹⁾) the value 0.9 is to be considered when stability against overturning or stress reversal is critical.
clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 1 (June 2001), p. 68, Table 18, col 4 (= WL under Limit State of Collapse): Substitute '−' for '1.0' against the Load Combination DL + IL.

Pre-amendment printed cell: row 'DL+IL', col 4 (Collapse-WL) = '1.0'. Post-amendment cell: '−' (i.e. not applicable). Structural reasoning: the row 'DL+IL' represents a load combination involving ONLY Dead Load and Imposed Load — by definition, NO Wind Load is included. Therefore the WL column for this row should be '−' (no value), not '1.0'. The '1.0' was an erroneous holdover from a uniform-filled-row formatting convention; multiplying a nonexistent wind load by 1.0 is meaningless. The Amendment No. 1 substitution corrects this to '−' to clearly indicate WL is absent from the DL+IL combination.

The 2021 BIS reprint of IS 456:2000 still prints '1.0' in this cell (verified by 200-dpi rasterisation of PDF p 69 / printed p 68) — the reprint did not incorporate this Amendment No. 1 hit. We encode the AMENDED reading per the cross-reference convention.

Other notable Table 18 features:
- Row 'DL+WL' col 2 (Collapse-DL) has TWO values: '1.5 or 0.9¹⁾'. The 0.9 value is used when stability against overturning or stress reversal is critical (footnote 1). This is the 'reduced dead-load' case where DL acts favourably (counterweight against overturning) — using a lower γ_f for DL gives a conservative check.
- Row 'DL+IL+WL' has γ_f = 1.2 for ALL three loads under Collapse — the three-load combination uses a smaller per-load factor (1.2 vs. 1.5) because simultaneous worst-case occurrence of all three loads is statistically less likely.
- Serviceability γ_f values are mostly 1.0 (i.e. characteristic load itself); for DL+IL+WL, IL and WL use 0.8 (recognising serviceability concerns are short-term and full simultaneous loading is rare).
- Note 2 specifies that serviceability values apply for SHORT-TERM effects; long-term creep uses DL + permanent fraction of IL only.

Interpolation: 'none_categorical' — the three load combinations are discrete categories with distinct γ_f sets; no interpolation between them.

Referenced by: clause 36.4.1 (this is the primary destination); clause 18.2.3.1 (Session 6 Batch 1 — load combinations); clause B-4.3 (Annex B, Session 7).

clauses.title: LSM — Partial safety factor γ_m for material strength: 36.4.2.1 collapse — 1.5 concrete / 1.15 steel; 36.4.2.2 deflection uses characteristic strength
clauses.text:
36.4.2.1 When assessing the strength of a structure or structural member for the limit state of collapse, the values of partial safety factor, γ_m, should be taken as 1.5 for concrete and 1.15 for steel.

NOTE — γ_m values are already incorporated in the equations and tables given in this standard for limit state design.

36.4.2.2 When assessing the deflection, the material properties such as modulus of elasticity should be taken as those associated with the characteristic strength of the material.
clauses.formula_latex: \gamma_m = \begin{cases} 1.5 & \text{concrete, limit state of collapse} \\ 1.15 & \text{steel, limit state of collapse} \\ 1.0 \text{ (effective)} & \text{deflection (use characteristic strength)} \end{cases}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Partial safety factor γ_m for material strength: 1.5 for concrete and 1.15 for steel, when assessing the limit state of collapse. For deflection assessment, use the material properties (e.g. E) associated with the CHARACTERISTIC strength — effectively γ_m = 1.0 for serviceability checks of deflection. NOTE: γ_m values are already incorporated in IS 456's design equations and tables — designers using those equations do not apply γ_m a second time.

variables.description: partial safety factor for material strength (output)

clauses.notes:
Two sub-clauses consolidated. (36.4.2.1) gives the two canonical γ_m values for collapse: 1.5 concrete, 1.15 steel. The NOTE is significant — IS 456's design equations (e.g. M_u,lim formulas in Annex G, the τ_c values in Table 19, the f_d-based formulas throughout) already have γ_m baked in. Designers should NOT apply γ_m again on top of these equations.

(36.4.2.2) is the deflection rule — material properties (modulus of elasticity E_c, E_s) for deflection calculations use the CHARACTERISTIC strength, not the design strength. This means γ_m = 1.0 effectively for the deflection check. The reasoning is that serviceability uses unfactored values; the factor of safety against deflection limits is built into the deflection limit (e.g. l/250) itself, not into the material properties.

Closing chunk for many forward references from prior sessions to 'γ_m = 1.5' or 'γ_m = 1.15'.

clauses.title: LSM — Analysis: methods per Section 22; characteristic-value material strengths for elastic properties; moment redistribution per 37.1.1
clauses.text: 37 Analysis. 37.1 Analysis of Structure — Methods of analysis as in 22 shall be used. The material strength to be assumed shall be characteristic values in the determination of elastic properties of members irrespective of the limit state being considered. Redistribution of the calculated moments may be made as given in 37.1.1.
clauses.notes:
Section 37 header + 37.1 consolidated. Three-sentence rule: (i) use Section 22 (Methods of Analysis, Session 3) for structural analysis; (ii) for elastic-property determination, use CHARACTERISTIC material strengths regardless of which limit state is being assessed (this is a counterintuitive but consistent rule — even when checking collapse, the analysis is performed with elastic properties based on characteristic strength, not design strength); (iii) moment redistribution per 37.1.1.

The rule that elastic properties use characteristic values (even for collapse analysis) means stiffness ratios (used in moment distribution) are NOT amplified by γ_m. Only the final-section-design step applies γ_m.

clauses.title: LSM — Moment redistribution in continuous beams and frames: 5 conditions (a-e) including 70 % moment-of-resistance floor, 30 % redistribution cap, x_u/d + δM/100 ≤ 0.6, 10 % cap for lateral-stability frames > 4 storeys
clauses.text:
The redistribution of moments may be carried out satisfying the following conditions:

(a) Equilibrium between the internal forces and the external loads is maintained.

(b) The ultimate moment of resistance provided at any section of a member is not less than 70 percent of the moment at that section obtained from an elastic maximum moment diagram covering all appropriate combinations of loads.

(c) The elastic moment at any section in a member due to a particular combination of loads shall not be reduced by more than 30 percent of the numerically largest moment given anywhere by the elastic maximum moments diagram for the particular member, covering all appropriate combination of loads.

(d) At sections where the moment capacity after redistribution is less than that from the elastic maximum moment diagram, the following relationship shall be satisfied:

(x_u / d) + (δM / 100) ≤ 0.6

where x_u = depth of neutral axis, d = effective depth, and δM = percentage reduction in moment.

(e) In structures in which the structural frame provides the lateral stability, the reductions in moment allowed by condition 37.1.1(c) shall be restricted to 10 percent for structures over 4 storeys in height.
clauses.formula_latex: (a) \, \text{Internal-external equilibrium maintained} \\ (b) \, M_{u, provided} \geq 0.70 \, M_{elastic, max} \\ (c) \, |M_{elastic} - M_{redistributed}| \leq 0.30 \, M_{elastic, max} \\ (d) \, \frac{x_u},{d} + \frac{\delta M},{100} \leq 0.6 \\ (e) \, \delta M_{max} \leq 10\% \text{ for lateral-stability frames} > 4 \text{ storeys}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: Moment redistribution in continuous beams and frames must satisfy five conditions. (a) Equilibrium between internal forces and external loads is preserved. (b) The ultimate moment of resistance at any section is at least 70% of the elastic maximum moment there. (c) The elastic moment at any section may be reduced by at most 30% of the numerically largest elastic moment in the member. (d) The 'ductility check': at sections where redistribution reduces capacity, the neutral-axis ratio x_u/d plus the percentage redistribution δM/100 must satisfy (x_u/d) + (δM/100) ≤ 0.6 — i.e. heavier redistribution requires more ductile sections (lower x_u/d). (e) For frames relying on structural action for lateral stability (i.e. unbraced frames) more than 4 storeys high, redistribution is capped at 10% (instead of 30%) — because excessive redistribution in tall unbraced frames compromises second-order stability.

variables.description: provided ultimate moment of resistance at a section (output)
numerically largest elastic moment in the member from all load combinations (input)
elastic moment at a specific section due to a particular load combination (input)
redistributed moment at the section (output)
depth of neutral axis at the section, after redistribution (input)
effective depth (input)
percentage reduction in moment at the section due to redistribution (output)

clauses.notes:
Closes many Session 3 (Section 22) forward references. Redistribution permits the designer to flatten moment peaks at supports (compensating with larger spans) — useful for placing reinforcement more economically and for handling moment-capacity mismatches.

The five conditions form a coherent set: (a) is the basic physics requirement. (b) and (c) are the 70%/30% reciprocal bounds — no section may have less than 70% of its elastic-required capacity, and no section's elastic moment may be reduced by more than 30% of the member's max elastic moment. (d) is the famous 'ductility check' — x_u/d is the relative neutral-axis depth, which encodes the section's ductility (low x_u/d = ductile, under-reinforced; high x_u/d = brittle, over-reinforced). δM/100 is the redistribution fraction. The sum bound 0.6 means: for a section with x_u/d = 0.48 (typical balanced/under-reinforced) only δM ≤ 12% redistribution is allowed; for very ductile sections (x_u/d = 0.3), up to 30% is allowed. (e) caps redistribution at 10% for unbraced frames > 4 storeys because second-order sway effects amplify the moment-redistribution sensitivity.

The 'lateral stability' clause (e) overrides condition (c) for tall unbraced frames — so a 10-storey moment-frame can only redistribute 10%, not 30%.

clauses.title: LSM Section 5 — Safety and Serviceability Requirements: section header + 35.1 General + 35.1.1 critical limit state + 35.1.2 characteristic values / design values via partial safety factors
clauses.text:
Section 5 of IS 456:2000 covers Structural Design by the Limit State Method (LSM). Section 35 sets the LSM safety and serviceability framework.

35.1 General — In the method of design based on limit state concept, the structure shall be designed to withstand safely all loads liable to act on it throughout its life; it shall also satisfy the serviceability requirements, such as limitations on deflection and cracking. The acceptable limit for the safety and serviceability requirements before failure occurs is called a 'limit state'. The aim of design is to achieve acceptable probabilities that the structure will not become unfit for the use for which it is intended, that is, that it will not reach a limit state.

35.1.1 All relevant limit states shall be considered in design to ensure an adequate degree of safety and serviceability. In general, the structure shall be designed on the basis of the most critical limit state and shall be checked for other limit states.

35.1.2 For ensuring the above objective, the design should be based on characteristic values for material strengths and applied loads, which take into account the variations in the material strengths and in the loads to be supported. The characteristic values should be based on statistical data if available; where such data are not available they should be based on experience. The 'design values' are derived from the characteristic values through the use of partial safety factors, one for material strengths and the other for loads. In the absence of special considerations these factors should have the values given in 36 according to the material, the type of loading and the limit state being considered.
clauses.notes:
LSM framework introduction. Three sub-clauses consolidated:

(35.1) defines 'limit state' as the acceptable boundary of safety + serviceability before failure; design aim is to achieve acceptable probabilities of not reaching any limit state.

(35.1.1) requires consideration of ALL relevant limit states; design on the most critical, check others.

(35.1.2) introduces the two-factor framework: characteristic values for materials and loads (statistical or experience-based), and design values derived via partial safety factors (γ_m for material, γ_f for load). Forward-references Section 36 for the actual factor values.

Member-type: `all-rcc-member` per Batch 8 brief — these are LSM method-level rules that apply to all RCC members.

Note: Section 35.3 (Limit States of Serviceability — Deflection + Cracking) was authored separately in Session 3 (chunk id presumably cl_35_3_serviceability_limits or similar). Section 35.3 is structurally part of Section 35 but was bundled with Session 3's deflection/cracking content. Cross-link added in see_also if Session 3 ID is known.

clauses.title: LSM — Limit State of Collapse: assessed from rupture, elastic/plastic buckling (incl. sway), or overturning; resistance ≥ effects from worst load combination
clauses.text: The limit state of collapse of the structure or part of the structure could be assessed from rupture of one or more critical sections and from buckling due to elastic or plastic instability (including the effects of sway where appropriate) or overturning. The resistance to bending, shear, torsion and axial loads at every section shall not be less than the appropriate value at that section produced by the probable most unfavourable combination of loads on the structure using the appropriate partial safety factors.
clauses.formula_latex: R_{section}(M, V, T, N) \geq E_{section}^{\max\_unfavourable}(\gamma_f \cdot F) \quad \text{at every section}
clauses.formula_nl_summary: The collapse limit state is assessed via three failure modes: (i) rupture of one or more critical sections; (ii) elastic or plastic buckling instability (including sway effects where relevant); (iii) overturning. At every section, the section's resistance to bending, shear, torsion, and axial load must be at least the demand produced by the most unfavourable load combination factored by the appropriate γ_f partial safety factors.

variables.description: section resistance to bending (M), shear (V), torsion (T), axial load (N) (output)
section effects from the most unfavourable factored load combination (input)
partial safety factor for loads per Table 18 (input)

clauses.notes:
Defines the three collapse failure modes (rupture, buckling, overturning) and the section-equilibrium criterion. The 'sway effects where appropriate' parenthetical covers second-order effects in unbraced frames — overlaps with Section 22 effective-length and slenderness rules.

The four resistance terms (bending, shear, torsion, axial) are the four force quantities that every section's design must check — this clause is the LSM analogue of Annex B's WSM strength check.

Member-type `all-rcc-member`: these are LSM method-level rules applicable to all members.

clauses.title: LSM — Other Limit States: unusual/special functions require additional limit-state compliance
clauses.text: Structures designed for unusual or special functions shall comply with any relevant additional limit state considered appropriate to that structure.
clauses.notes: Short open-ended clause covering structures beyond the standard residential / commercial scope — e.g. fatigue limit state for structures with cyclic loading (cranes, bridges), fire-resistance time limits, vibration / serviceability limits for sensitive equipment foundations. The clause does not enumerate these but invites the designer to add appropriate checks. Section 35 is therefore a 3-state framework: 35.2 Collapse, 35.3 Serviceability (Session 3), 35.4 Other.

clauses.title: LSM — Characteristic and Design Values and Partial Safety Factors: section header
clauses.text: Section 36 sets the characteristic / design value framework that converts statistical material strengths and loads into design quantities via partial safety factors. Coverage: 36.1 Characteristic Strength of Materials; 36.2 Characteristic Loads; 36.3 Design Values (with sub-clauses for materials, loads, and consequences); 36.4 Partial Safety Factors (with γ_f for loads per Table 18 and γ_m for material strength per 36.4.2).
clauses.notes: Section 36 header chunk. This section is the most-referenced section of IS 456 across the corpus — Sessions 1-5 contain dozens of forward references to 'γ_f', 'γ_m', 'partial safety factors', 'Table 18', and 'characteristic strength' that all resolve here.

clauses.title: Design per IS 456 with IS 13920 overrides for LFRS elements
clauses.text: The design and construction of RC buildings shall be governed by the provisions of IS 456, except as modified by the provisions of this code for those elements participating in lateral force resistance.
clauses.notes:
Clause 5.1 establishes the parent-and-override relationship between IS 13920:2016 and IS 456:2000. The default rule is full conformance with IS 456 for design and construction; this code's seismic-specific provisions act as overrides on IS 456 specifically for elements that participate in lateral force resistance (i.e., elements of the LFRS as defined in clause 3.7).

Editorial note (cross-corpus parallel). The cross-corpus see_also to 'cl_18_1_aim_of_design' anchors the parent design-aim umbrella in IS 456. The Section 18 family of IS 456 establishes the overall design methodology (limit-state method as default, working-stress method as alternative) that this clause inherits by reference. The IS 456 reference itself is canonically resolved through the Section 2 referred-standards table; an explicit see_also to that table is not added here per the IS 456 convention of restricting reference-table see_alos to chunks that consume a specific row of the table.

clauses.title: Minimum grade of structural concrete — M20 default, M25 for tall buildings in Zones III/IV/V
clauses.text: The minimum grade of structural concrete in any RC member shall be M20. The minimum rises to M25 in buildings of height greater than 15 m located in Seismic Zone III, IV, or V. In all cases, the minimum grade of structural concrete shall not fall below the exposure-driven minimum required by IS 456 (Table 5 of IS 456) for the exposure condition applicable to the structure.
clauses.notes:
Clause 5.2 establishes two minimum-grade rules that act together as a maximum-of:

(i) The seismic-driven minimum — M20 generally, raised to M25 for buildings taller than 15 m in Seismic Zones III, IV, V. This rule is internal to IS 13920.

(ii) The exposure-driven minimum — not less than the minimum grade required by IS 456 Table 5 for the applicable exposure condition (mild/moderate/severe/very severe/extreme). This rule is external to IS 13920, invoked here by reference. For severe-or-worse exposure conditions, the IS 456 Table 5 minimum may exceed M25, in which case the IS 456 minimum governs.

The operative minimum in any specific design is the larger of (i) and (ii). The chunk text encodes both rules; the rules layer should evaluate both and take the maximum.

Three cross-corpus see_alos anchor the IS 456 dependencies: 'cl_6_1_grades' is the parent definition of concrete grades (M-numbers) in IS 456; 'table_2_concrete_grades' is the IS 456 lookup table of grade designations to characteristic compressive strengths; 'table_5_minimum_cement_max_wc_min_grade' is the IS 456 exposure-driven minimum-grade table invoked by rule (ii) above.

clauses.title: Steel reinforcement in earthquake-resisting RC members — umbrella
clauses.text: Steel reinforcement used to resist earthquake-induced forces in RC frame members, and in boundary elements of RC structural walls, shall comply with the requirements of clauses 5.3.1, 5.3.2, and 5.3.3.
clauses.notes:
Umbrella clause for the 5.3 cluster (steel reinforcement requirements). The umbrella text references three sub-clauses (5.3.1, 5.3.2, 5.3.3), of which two remain post-Amendment No. 2 as authored corpus chunks: 'cl_13920_5_3_1_steel_requirements' (post-Am-2 wholesale rewrite into a four-bullet form) and 'cl_13920_5_3_2_actual_vs_characteristic_stress' (post-Am-2 stress-for-strength wording fix).

Editorial inference (necessary fiction) — stale internal reference to 5.3.3 post-Amendment No. 2. Amendment No. 2 (November 2020) deletes clause 5.3.3 in its entirety but does NOT amend the 5.3 umbrella sentence, which retains its printed reference to '5.3.1, 5.3.2 and 5.3.3'. The chunk text encodes the printed reading literally, including the now-stale reference to 5.3.3. The substantive content of the deleted 5.3.3 (the ≥ 1.15 lower bound on the ultimate-stress-to-proof-stress ratio) is absorbed into post-Am-2 5.3.1 bullet (c); the umbrella's reference to 5.3.3 therefore points to a now-empty clause whose content lives in 5.3.1(c). Flagged here as a necessary fiction so the rules layer can audit the inference: post-Am-2, a query that follows the umbrella's '5.3.3' reference will resolve to nothing in the corpus, and the rules layer should instead resolve to 5.3.1 bullet (c) to recover the deleted content. This is a candidate for the cross-section amendment correction registry (decisions log entry #2).

clauses.title: Steel reinforcement requirements — four-bullet specification (post-Amendment No. 2)
clauses.text: Steel reinforcement used to resist earthquake-induced forces shall comply with all four of the following requirements concurrently: (a) the total elongation shall be at least 14.5 percent; (b) the ratio of the actual ultimate stress to the actual 0.2 percent proof stress shall not exceed 1.25; (c) the ratio of the actual ultimate stress to the actual 0.2 percent proof stress shall be at least 1.15; and (d) the steel shall be of a strength grade with minimum 0.2 percent proof stress of 415 MPa, 500 MPa, or 550 MPa, in addition to the other requirements specified in IS 1786.
clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 2 (November 2020), p. 3, clause 5.3.1: substitutes clause 5.3.1 in its entirety with the four-bullet form encoded here. Pre-amendment printed reading: 'Steel reinforcements used shall be, a) of grade Fe 415 or less (conforming to IS 1786); and b) of grade Fe 500 and Fe 550, that is; high strength deformed steel bars produced by thermo-mechanical treatment process having elongation more than 14.5 percent, and conforming to IS 1786.' Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is the four-bullet form: (a) elongation shall be at least 14.5 percent; (b) ratio of ultimate stress to 0.2 percent proof stress shall not exceed 1.25; (c) ratio of ultimate stress to 0.2 percent proof stress shall be at least 1.15; (d) steel shall be only of strength grades with minimum 0.2 percent proof stress of 415, 500, or 550 MPa, in addition to other requirements of IS 1786. The substitution restructures the original grade-list specification into a four-attribute property specification that applies uniformly across all permitted strength grades. Three substantive shifts result: first, the 14.5 percent elongation threshold is now mandatory for all permitted grades, whereas the pre-amendment reading made it a qualifier specifically for Fe 500/550; second, the ratio bounds on actual ultimate stress to actual proof stress are now stated in 5.3.1 itself (with the lower bound of 1.15 absorbed from the deleted 5.3.3 — see the next paragraph — and the upper bound of 1.25 introduced new — see paragraph three); third, the grade allow-list is restated as a minimum-proof-stress allow-list of 415, 500, or 550 MPa, with IS 1786 conformance preserved as an in-addition-to clause.

Amendment No. 2 (November 2020), p. 4, clause 5.3.3: deletes clause 5.3.3 in its entirety. Pre-deletion printed reading of 5.3.3: 'The ratio of the actual ultimate strength to the actual 0.2 percent proof strength shall be at least 1.15.' Post-deletion: no separate 5.3.3 chunk exists in the post-Am-2 corpus. The deleted requirement is not retired from the substantive content of the code — it is consolidated into post-Am-2 5.3.1 bullet (c), which states the same ≥ 1.15 lower bound on the ratio of actual ultimate stress to actual 0.2 percent proof stress. The wording in bullet (c) substitutes 'stress' for 'strength' on both occurrences relative to the pre-deletion 5.3.3 text, paralleling the Am-2 wording fix applied to clause 5.3.2; the engineering content (≥ 1.15 ratio floor) is preserved verbatim. The deletion of 5.3.3 is therefore a consolidation move into the post-Am-2 5.3.1 four-bullet form, not a retirement of the requirement, and no separate post-Am-2 5.3.3 chunk is authored in this corpus.

Editorial note on the Amendment No. 2 sweep over the 5.3 cluster. Bullet (b) of post-Am-2 5.3.1 — the ≤ 1.25 upper cap on the ratio of actual ultimate stress to actual 0.2 percent proof stress — is new to IS 13920 in Amendment No. 2; it does not appear in pre-amendment 5.3.1, in the deleted 5.3.3, or in any other clause of pre-amendment IS 13920:2016. The engineering rationale for the new upper cap is to constrain the ductile-mechanism design: an excessively high ratio (large gap between proof stress and ultimate stress) implies a steel with high strain-hardening capacity but uncertain yield behaviour, which can shift the design plastic-hinge formation away from the intended ductile mechanism and can also cause beam-column joint moments to exceed the design envelope under inelastic action. The companion Amendment No. 2 substitution at clause 5.3.2 (substituting 'stress' for 'strength' on lines 1 and 3) is a wording fix only — the engineering content is unchanged — and reflects the same coherent rewrite of the 5.3 cluster terminology. The 5.3.2 chunk carries its own full Amendment No. 2 paragraph in its own notes field; the cross-link mentioned here is engineering-context only.

clauses.title: Actual 0.2% proof stress versus characteristic 0.2% proof stress — bound (post-Amendment No. 2)
clauses.text: The actual 0.2 percent proof stress of steel bars, determined from a tensile test on a representative specimen, shall not exceed the characteristic 0.2 percent proof stress of the steel grade by more than 20 percent.
clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 2 (November 2020), p. 4, clause 5.3.2, lines 1 and 3: substitutes 'stress' for 'strength' (two occurrences). Pre-amendment printed reading: 'The actual 0.2 percent proof strength of steel bars based on tensile test must not exceed their characteristic 0.2 percent proof strength by more than 20 percent.' Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is: 'The actual 0.2 percent proof stress of steel bars based on tensile test must not exceed their characteristic 0.2 percent proof stress by more than 20 percent.' The substitution is a wording fix only; the engineering content — the 20 percent upper bound of the actual proof reading over the characteristic proof reading — is unchanged. 'Stress' is the state variable describing the force-per-unit-area condition of the material at a specific test event; 'strength' is the property of the material describing its limit-state capacity. The 0.2 percent proof value of a tensile-tested specimen is a stress reading at a specific strain offset (per IS 1786 §3.4), not a strength property; the Amendment No. 2 wording fix aligns the IS 13920 terminology with the locked engineering meaning and with IS 1786's canonical use of '0.2 percent proof stress'. The companion Am-2 substitution in clause 5.3.1 (post-rewrite) and the absorbed 5.3.3 deletion content also use 'stress' rather than 'strength' for the same parameter, completing the consistent treatment across the 5.3 cluster.

Editorial note (engineering significance of the wording fix). Although the substitution does not change substantive engineering content, it matters for the rules layer's downstream interpretation. The pre-amendment 'proof strength' phrasing was an internally inconsistent IS 13920 artifact: IS 1786 (the parent material standard cited in 5.3.1 and in the Section 2 referred-standards table) consistently uses '0.2 percent proof stress' as the canonical parameter name for the post-yield tensile reading at 0.2 percent strain offset. The pre-Am-2 IS 13920 wording was a stylistic drift from IS 1786; post-Am-2 5.3.2 restores conformance with IS 1786's terminology. The rules layer should treat queries for 'proof stress' and (legacy) 'proof strength' as resolving to the same physical parameter when the source is IS 13920, but should prefer 'proof stress' as the canonical query term in alignment with IS 1786 and post-Am-2 IS 13920.

clauses.title: Analytical-model inclusion of lintel, plinth, and staircase elements; short-column avoidance
clauses.text: In RC frame buildings, lintel beams shall preferably not be integrated into the columns, to avoid the short-column effect that arises when a lintel beam framing rigidly into a column reduces the column's effective shear span. Where integration is unavoidable, the integrated lintel beam shall be included in the analytical model used for structural analysis. The same modelling obligation extends to plinth beams (where provided), and to staircase beams and slabs that frame into columns: all such elements shall be included in the analytical model for structural analysis.
clauses.notes:
Clause 5.4 governs the inclusion of secondary RC elements in the structural analytical model. Two policy positions sit in this clause:

(i) A detailing preference — lintel beams should preferably not be rigidly integrated into columns, because the short-column effect that results from a lintel framing into a column shortens the column's effective shear span and drives premature shear failure under cyclic seismic demand.

(ii) An analytical-modelling obligation — when integration is unavoidable (for lintel beams) or structurally always present (for plinth beams, staircase beams, staircase slabs framing into columns), the integrated element shall be included in the structural analytical model. The intent is to capture the actual stiffness and deformation paths of the structure rather than an idealised LFRS-only model that omits these secondary contributors.

Reciprocal link to clause 1.1.3 (lateral-force partitioning). Clause 1.1.3 carries the companion analytical-modelling obligation on members that the designer assumes do NOT participate in the LFRS: their effect on the seismic response of the system shall be accounted for. Clause 5.4 is the operational complement — it specifies which secondary elements (lintel, plinth, staircase) must be included in the analytical model regardless of the LFRS-partitioning choice. The bidirectional see_also between 1.1.3 and 5.4 makes the analytical-model-completeness obligation traceable across the code. (This reciprocity requires a retro edit to Batch 1's 1.1.3 chunk to add the back-edge to 5.4 — recorded as the explicit retro item in Batch 4 decisions log §6.)

clauses.title: Planar frame orientation and irregularity avoidance (post-Amendment No. 1)
clauses.text: RC regular moment-resisting frame buildings shall have planar frames oriented along the two principal plan directions of the building. Irregularities listed in IS 1893 (Part 1) shall be avoided. Buildings with any of the listed irregularities perform poorly during earthquake shaking; in addition, buildings with floating columns and set-back columns also perform poorly. When irregularities appear in a building as listed in Tables 5 or 6 of IS 1893 (Part 1), the guidance given therein for the respective irregularity in Tables 5 or 6 shall be followed.
clauses.notes:
Amendment No. 1 (September 2017), p. 4, clause 5.5, last sentence: substitutes the closing sentence of clause 5.5. Pre-amendment printed reading of the last sentence: 'When any such irregularities are adopted, detailed nonlinear analyses shall be performed to demonstrate that there is no threat to loss of life and property.' Post-amendment reading — encoded here — is: 'When irregularities appear in a building as listed in Tables 5 or 6 of IS 1893 (Part 1), the guidance given therein for the respective irregularity in Tables 5 or 6 shall be followed.' The substitution converts the closing sentence from an operative performance-demonstration obligation (perform a nonlinear analysis to demonstrate no threat to life and property when irregularities are adopted) to an operative cross-reference obligation (follow the per-irregularity guidance codified in IS 1893 (Part 1) Tables 5 and 6). The post-amendment reading delegates the engineering judgement on irregularity treatment from a bespoke nonlinear analysis performed by the designer to the codified Table 5 and Table 6 guidance of IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 (the binding post-Am-1 edition of IS 1893 (Part 1) per clause 2 of this code).

Editorial note (brief discrepancy on 'last sentence' identification — surfaced to hub for verification). The Batch 4 authoring brief identified the pre-Am-1 'last sentence' of 5.5 as 'Buildings with any of the listed irregularities perform poorly during earthquake shaking; in addition, buildings with floating columns and set-back columns also perform poorly.' (descriptive). Direct inspection of the printed IS 13920:2016 standard (reaffirmed-2021 consolidated edition, page 4) shows that clause 5.5 has four sentences in two-column print layout: the first three sentences fill the bottom of the left column ending at '...also perform poorly', and the fourth sentence sits at the top of the right column reading 'When any such irregularities are adopted, detailed nonlinear analyses shall be performed to demonstrate that there is no threat to loss of life and property.' A column-order reading of the printed page makes the fourth sentence (the 'nonlinear analyses' sentence) the visually-last sentence of clause 5.5, not the third sentence (the 'perform poorly' sentence). The Amendment No. 1 directive's literal phrase 'last sentence' therefore refers to sentence four. The encoding here follows that interpretation — sentence three ('perform poorly') is retained in the post-amendment reading, and sentence four ('nonlinear analyses') is replaced by the new operative cross-reference. If the brief's interpretation is correct and Amendment No. 1 in fact intended to replace sentence three (the descriptive 'perform poorly' sentence), the chunk text and the pre-amendment quoted reading in the lead paragraph above will need a one-paragraph revision — drop sentence three from the post-amendment chunk text and revise the pre-amendment quote in the Am-1 lead. Hub to verify against the BIS authoritative source.

Editorial note (external reference to IS 1893 (Part 1) Tables 5 and 6). The post-Am-1 last sentence resolves to IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 Tables 5 (vertical irregularities — soft storey, weak storey, mass irregularity, vertical-geometric irregularity, in-plane discontinuity, etc.) and Table 6 (plan irregularities — torsional irregularity, re-entrant corners, diaphragm discontinuity, out-of-plane offsets, non-parallel systems). The IS 1893 (Part 1):2016 edition reference is the post-Am-1 canonical edition per clause 2 of this code (see 'table_13920_2_referred_standards', row 4). No intra-corpus see_also link is added from this chunk to the references table, following the IS 456 convention of restricting reference-table see_alos to chunks that consume a specific row's content directly (which is not the relationship here — 5.5 invokes Tables 5 and 6 of IS 1893 (Part 1), not the listing itself).

clauses.title: Section 6 — Beams — umbrella; applicability bound by axial-stress threshold (0.08 f_ck)
clauses.text: The provisions of Section 6 apply to RC beams that resist earthquake-induced effects and in which the factored axial compressive stress does not exceed 0.08 times the characteristic compressive strength of concrete (0.08 f_ck). A beam in which the factored axial compressive stress exceeds 0.08 f_ck shall not be designed under the provisions of this section; the designer shall instead apply the requirements of Section 7 (Columns and Inclined Members) to such a member.
clauses.notes:
Umbrella chunk for Section 6 (Beams), folding the Section 6 heading and clause 6.1 General into one chunk per the Session 1 Batch 1 precedent of folding Section 1 heading + clause 1.1 Scope. The umbrella carries the operative content of 6.1 General — the 0.08 f_ck axial-stress threshold that bounds the applicability of Section 6 — rather than acting as a pure heading chunk.

Editorial note (the 0.08 f_ck threshold as the corpus's first member-classification rule). The 0.08 f_ck threshold is the first explicit member-classification rule in the IS 13920 corpus: it draws a load-based line between 'beam treated as beam under Section 6' and 'beam-like member treated as column under Section 7'. The threshold is load-based (compressive stress under factored load combinations) rather than geometric (cross-sectional proportions); this distinguishes it from IS 456's geometric column/strut definition at clause 25.1.1 (cross-corpus see_also), which classifies by the effective-length-to-least-lateral-dimension ratio. The two definitions are conceptually parallel — both define when a member transitions from beam-dominant to column-dominant design treatment — but use different criteria appropriate to their respective scopes (IS 456 governs general RC design; IS 13920 governs seismic design where axial loads on flexural members can fluctuate sharply under earthquake action).

Editorial note (retrievability of the threshold rule). The folding decision (umbrella + 6.1 General as one chunk) places the substantive threshold rule inside the umbrella rather than in a dedicated chunk. Retrievability is preserved through the tags 'axial-stress-limit' (applied here and on cl_13920_7_1_columns_umbrella when authored in Batch 4 — that chunk carries the parallel 0.40 f_ck axial-stress limit for column design) and 'member-classification' (new tag introduced in this chunk; will be applied to other chunks where the code distinguishes member types by load or geometric conditions). A query for the 0.08 f_ck threshold should resolve to this chunk via the tag combination plus chunk-text matching.

clauses.title: Width-to-depth ratio preference (b/D > 0.3)
clauses.text: Beams shall preferably have a width-to-depth ratio greater than 0.3, that is, the beam width b should be more than three-tenths of the beam depth D.
clauses.notes:
Clause 6.1.1 expresses a preference, not a hard requirement: the directive verb is 'shall preferably' rather than 'shall'. Engineering practice should aim for b/D > 0.3 but the threshold is not absolute; designers may justify lower ratios with engineering calculations under the general design judgement permitted by the parent code IS 456.

Editorial note (engineering rationale for the 0.3 floor). Beams with very low width-to-depth ratios — that is, narrow and deep beams — are prone to lateral-torsional instability under the cyclic flexural action induced by earthquake loading. The 0.3 floor is a proportions-based heuristic that keeps beams stocky enough to develop their full plastic-hinge moment capacity without lateral-torsional buckling becoming the governing failure mode. The 'preferably' language reflects that the heuristic is informed by typical RC beam geometries in moment-resisting frames; very wide beams (high b/D) or specially-detailed narrow beams may both be acceptable with appropriate engineering justification.

clauses.title: Minimum beam width — 200 mm
clauses.text: Beams shall not have a width less than 200 mm. The 200 mm minimum applies to the cross-section width measured perpendicular to the beam's longitudinal axis.
clauses.notes:
Hard minimum — the directive verb is 'shall', not 'shall preferably' as in clause 6.1.1. Engineering practice has no discretion to fall below the 200 mm minimum width; a beam with width less than 200 mm does not satisfy this code and the design is non-conforming.

Editorial note (engineering rationale for the 200 mm floor). The 200 mm width minimum is set primarily by detailing constraints rather than by structural strength considerations: the minimum width must accommodate two layers of longitudinal reinforcement (top and bottom of the beam, per clause 6.2.1), the closed-loop transverse reinforcement (links / stirrups around the perimeter, per clause 6.3.1), the clear cover to reinforcement (per IS 456 §26.4), and adequate concrete placement and compaction space between the closely-spaced links in regions of close transverse reinforcement (per clause 6.3.5). Below 200 mm, detailing the section per these requirements becomes geometrically infeasible.

clauses.title: Maximum depth-to-clear-span ratio — D ≤ ¼ × clear span (with water-tank-staging exception)
clauses.text: Beams shall not have a depth D greater than one-quarter of their clear span. The rule may be waived for the floor beam of the frame staging of elevated RC water tanks, where the deep-beam geometry that results from the support-spacing layout is inherent to the staging design and an alternative beam configuration is not feasible.
clauses.notes:
Clause 6.1.3 imposes an upper bound on beam depth relative to clear span — equivalently, a lower bound on the clear-span-to-depth ratio of 4. Beams deeper than ¼ of their clear span are deep-beam-like in their behaviour: shear deformation becomes significant relative to flexural deformation, plane-sections-remain-plane breaks down, and the plastic-hinge model that underpins ductile seismic detailing (per clause 6.3.3) does not predict the shear capacity reliably. The rule keeps beams within the slender-flexural regime where the seismic detailing rules of Sections 6.2, 6.3 apply.

Editorial note (water-tank-staging exception). The exception is a geometry-driven concession: the floor beam of the frame staging of an elevated RC water tank typically has a depth dictated by the load it carries (the water tank and its contents) and a span set by the spacing of the staging columns, with the result that the depth-to-clear-span ratio can exceed ¼ as a consequence of the staging configuration. The exception does not remove the seismic-detailing obligations from such a beam; it only waives the proportions rule. The remaining Section 6 rules continue to apply.

Editorial note (cross-corpus parallel at IS 456 §23.3). The cross-corpus see_also to 'cl_23_3_slenderness_limits' anchors the related beam-proportions rule in the parent code. IS 456 §23.3 bounds the unbraced length of a beam between lateral restraints (governing lateral-torsional stability under gravity loads); IS 13920 §6.1.3 bounds the depth-to-clear-span ratio of a beam (governing the validity of the slender-flexural model under cyclic seismic loads). The two rules are complementary structural-proportions constraints with different governing concerns; both should be checked in design.

clauses.title: Beam width bound by supporting column dimensions (b_w ≤ c_2 + min(c_2, 0.75 c_1) on each side)
clauses.text: The width of the beam b_w shall not exceed the width of the supporting member c_2 plus, on either side of the supporting member, a distance equal to the smaller of (a) the width of the supporting member, c_2; and (b) 0.75 times the breadth of the supporting member, c_1 (see Fig. 1A and Fig. 1B). For the case of a wide beam — that is, a beam whose width exceeds the width of the supporting column c_2 — the transverse reinforcement covering the portion of the beam width that lies outside the column width shall be provided as shown in Fig. 1B, throughout the beam span including within the beam-column joint.
clauses.notes:
Clause 6.1.4 carries two distinct rules in two consecutive paragraphs of the printed standard (with Fig 1 sitting between the two paragraphs in the printed two-column layout, page 4). Both rules are encoded in this one chunk because both belong structurally to clause 6.1.4 — the first paragraph states the geometric bound on beam width relative to the supporting column, and the second paragraph states the consequent transverse-reinforcement obligation when the bound permits the beam to be wider than the supporting column.

Editorial note (inline variables and the absence of a formula_latex block). The chunk uses inline variable names b_w (beam width), c_2 (the supporting column dimension perpendicular to the beam axis — that is, the width of the column measured along the axis perpendicular to the beam), and c_1 (the supporting column dimension parallel to the beam axis — that is, the column dimension measured along the beam's longitudinal axis). The variable conventions match Fig 1's labelled dimensions. No 'formula_latex' block is added here because the rule is a verbal bound (an inequality with a min operator) rather than a closed-form computational formula; per the variables-block convention boundary locked at the close of Session 1, variables blocks are reserved for chunks that carry a 'formula_latex' field, which begins at clause 6.2.1 (ρ_min) in Batch 2.

Editorial note (the wide-beam transverse-reinforcement obligation). The second-paragraph rule extends transverse reinforcement coverage beyond the supporting-column footprint when the beam is wide relative to the column. The intent is to maintain the closed-loop confinement of the beam's longitudinal bars across the full beam width — including the portion that overhangs the column — so that the plastic-hinge region adjacent to the beam-column joint develops uniformly across the section. Fig 1B shows the layout of the extended transverse reinforcement; the chunk's see_also to fig_13920_1_beam_column_junction reciprocates.

clauses.title: Fig. 1 — Beam-column junctions (1A plan view of joint effective breadth and width; 1B maximum effective width of wide beam and required transverse reinforcement)
clauses.nl_caption: Two-panel figure depicting beam-column joint geometry. Panel 1A is a plan view of a rectangular beam-column joint that shows the effective breadth and width of the joint, with the column dimensions labelled c_1 (along the beam axis) and c_2 (perpendicular to the beam axis) and the beam width b_w marked over the joint footprint. Panel 1B is a section view (cut perpendicular to the beam axis) of the maximum-permitted wide-beam configuration, where the beam width b_w exceeds the column width c_2; the panel shows the closed-loop transverse reinforcement extending across the full beam width on each side of the column, with the per-side overhang of the beam-over-column bounded by the smaller of c_2 and 0.75 × c_1 as specified in clause 6.1.4. Section A-A through Panel 1B indicates the orientation of the closed-loop transverse reinforcement relative to the beam's longitudinal axis.
clauses.notes:
Figure chunk for IS 13920:2016 Fig. 1, located on printed page 4 of the standard. The figure is referenced by clause 6.1.4 (the only IS 13920 clause that cites Fig. 1A or Fig. 1B). The reciprocal see_also from 'cl_13920_6_1_4_beam_width_vs_column_width' anchors the figure to its operative clause.

Pattern-lock note (first figure chunk in the IS 13920 corpus). This chunk pattern-locks the figure-chunk shape for the corpus: required fields are 'id', 'content_type', 'code', 'clause', 'page', 'figure_number', 'figure_subtype', 'title', 'nl_caption', 'image_url', 'tags', 'category', 'status', 'see_also'; optional 'notes'. No 'text' field, no 'caption' field, no formula fields. The 'figure_subtype' value is a bespoke snake_case descriptor specific to this figure's content ('beam_column_joint_plan_section'), matching the bespoke-descriptive pattern of IS 456's 21 figure chunks. The 'image_url' field is set to null until Phase 3 visual asset population.

Editorial note (no amendment provenance on Fig 1). Neither Amendment No. 1 (September 2017) nor Amendment No. 2 (November 2020) modifies Fig. 1. Per the implicit-absence convention locked in Session 1, no 'no amendments hit' declaration is added to this chunk's notes; the absence of an amendment lead is itself the signal.

clauses.title: Quality Assurance Measures — purpose, technical/organizational dimensions, and the Quality Assurance Plan
clauses.text:
(10.1.1) In order that the properties of the completed structure be consistent with the requirements and the assumptions made during planning and design, adequate quality assurance measures shall be taken. The construction should result in satisfactory strength, serviceability, and long-term durability so as to lower the overall life-cycle cost. Quality assurance in construction activity relates to:

— proper design;
— use of adequate materials and components, to be supplied by the producers;
— proper workmanship in the execution of works by the contractor; and
— ultimately, proper care during use of the structure including timely maintenance and repair by the owner.

(10.1.2) Quality assurance measures are both technical and organizational. Some common cases should be specified in a general Quality Assurance Plan which shall identify the key elements necessary to provide fitness of the structure and the means by which they are to be provided and measured, with the overall purpose to provide confidence that the realized project will work satisfactorily in service fulfilling intended needs. The job of quality control and quality assurance involves quality audit of both inputs and outputs. Inputs are: materials for concrete; workmanship in all stages of batching, mixing, transportation, placing, compaction, and curing; and the related plant, machinery and equipment. The output is concrete in place. To ensure proper performance, each step in concreting which will be covered by the next step shall be inspected as the work proceeds (see also Section 17).

(10.1.3) Each party involved in the realization of a project shall establish and implement a Quality Assurance Plan for its participation in the project. Supplier's and subcontractor's activities shall be covered in the plan. The individual QA Plans shall fit into the general QA Plan. A QA Plan shall define:

— the tasks and responsibilities of all persons involved;
— adequate control and checking procedures; and
— the organization and maintenance of adequate documentation of the building process and its results.

Such documentation should generally include: (a) test reports and manufacturer's certificate for materials, concrete-mix design details; (b) pour cards for site organization and clearance for concrete placement; (c) record of site inspection of workmanship and field tests; (d) non-conformance reports, change orders; (e) quality control charts; (f) statistical analysis. Note: quality control charts are recommended wherever the concrete is in continuous production over considerable periods.
clauses.notes: No amendments touch clauses 10.1, 10.1.1, 10.1.2, or 10.1.3. Bundles parent 10.1 with all three sub-clauses because the content reads as a single QA-plan umbrella rule and none of the sub-clauses carries an amendment that would warrant standalone treatment. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause about project-wide quality-assurance discipline, applicable regardless of structural member type. Forward reference to Section 17 (inspection) is intra-Session-5 and resolves at session-completion two-pass.

clauses.title: Batching — general principles, ingredient measurement, volume-batching fallback, and substitution prohibition
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): substitutes paragraph 2 of clause 10.2 with the RMC-preferred / large-medium-project-mandate text encoded here. The pre-amendment para 2 mentioned only that RMC supplied by RMC plant shall be preferred; the Amendment No. 4 form makes RMC (or captive on-site/off-site automatic batching plants) effectively mandatory for large and medium projects, citing IS 4926 for the supplied concrete and requiring equivalent quality control for the on-site/off-site automatic plants. Bundles parent 10.2 (header + general batching para) with 10.2.4 (volume-batching fallback rule) and 10.2.6 (substitution prohibition) — none of 10.2.4 or 10.2.6 carries an amendment of its own, and all three read as a single 'general batching discipline' umbrella. The Amendment No. 4-substituted clauses (10.2.1 first sentence, 10.2.2 full clause, 10.2.3 full clause, 10.2.5 fourth-sentence insertion) are split out as standalone chunks because each carries an amendment-distinct rule. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing batching operations at the plant or site, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Aggregate grading control — separate stockpiles and blending discipline
clauses.text:
The grading of aggregate shall be controlled by obtaining the coarse aggregate in different sizes and blending them in the right proportions, the different sizes being stocked in separate stock-piles.

The material should be stock-piled for several hours, preferably a day, before use. The grading of coarse and fine aggregate should be checked as frequently as possible — the frequency for a given job being determined by the engineer-in-charge to ensure that the specified grading is maintained.

Without the separate-stock-piles discipline, mixed-size stockpiles segregate during handling (coarser fractions migrating to the base, finer to the top) and yield uncontrolled grading at the batching point. The pre-blend-at-batching approach forces grading to be the engineering output of a deliberate proportioning step rather than an accident of stockpile state.
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): substitutes the first sentence of clause 10.2.1. The pre-amendment first sentence had a long conditional opening — 'Except where it can be shown to the satisfaction of the engineer-in-charge that supply of properly graded aggregate of uniform quality can be maintained over a period of work, the grading of aggregate should be controlled by obtaining the coarse aggregate in different sizes and blending them in the right proportions...'. The Amendment No. 4 substitution removes the conditional escape clause and makes the separate-stock-piles-and-blend discipline unconditional. The remainder of the clause (stock-pile-for-hours-preferably-a-day, frequency of grading checks) is unchanged. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing aggregate handling at the batching plant or site.

clauses.title: Measuring equipment — accuracy tolerances and periodic calibration intervals
clauses.text:
The accuracy of the measuring equipment shall be:

— within ± 2 percent of the quantity of cement and mineral admixtures being measured;
— within ± 3 percent of the quantity of aggregate, chemical admixtures, and water being measured.

In a batching plant, the concrete production equipment shall be calibrated initially at the time of installation or reconditioning of the equipment, and subsequently at the following intervals:

(a) Mechanical / knife-edge systems: at least once every two months
(b) Electrical / load-cell systems: at least once every three months

The ± 2 percent / ± 3 percent split tracks the materials whose dosage most strongly drives strength and durability: cement and mineral admixtures (which together set the binder content and the supplementary-cementitious-material proportion) carry the tighter limit, while aggregates (whose grading and moisture variation already introduces other tolerances) and admixtures and water carry the looser limit.
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): full substitution of clause 10.2.2. The pre-amendment clause specified only the accuracy tolerances — ± 2 percent for cement, ± 3 percent for aggregate, admixtures, and water — without mention of mineral admixtures or calibration intervals. The Amendment No. 4 form (a) adds mineral admixtures to the tight ± 2 percent group (consistent with the Amendment No. 5 elevation of mineral admixtures in clause 5.2) and (b) introduces the periodic-calibration discipline distinguishing mechanical/knife-edge from electrical/load-cell systems. Standalone chunk because the calibration-interval rule is a distinct amendment-introduced obligation with its own variables (calibration intervals indexed by equipment type). No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing batching-plant equipment accuracy and calibration, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Mass-only proportioning — exception for water and chemical admixtures
clauses.text:
All ingredients of the concrete shall be used by mass, except water and chemical admixtures, which may be measured by volume.

The rule operationalizes the more general principle stated in clause 10.2 (cement and aggregate by mass; admixture by mass if solid, by mass or volume if liquid; water by mass or calibrated volume): every solid ingredient is by mass; the only volume-allowed ingredients are water and chemical admixtures (both of which can be liquid and which the code separately covers by the 'calibrated tank' / liquid-admixture provisions in clause 10.2 and the IS 4925 reference).
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): full substitution of clause 10.2.3. The pre-amendment clause read 'Proportion/Type and grading of aggregates shall be made by trial in such a way so as to obtain densest possible concrete. All ingredients of the concrete should be used by mass only.' The Amendment No. 4 form (encoded here) drops the densest-concrete sentence — that requirement was relocated to clause 9.2.2 by the same Amendment No. 4 (insertion after first sentence of 9.2.2) — and restates the all-by-mass rule with the explicit exception for water and chemical admixtures, which is the only point that requires reading. Standalone chunk because Amendment No. 4 makes 10.2.3 a distinct rule about which-ingredients-by-mass, separate from the densest-concrete trial-proportioning rule which now sits in 9.2.2. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing batching practice, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Moisture-content compensation — surface-water adjustment and probe-based monitoring at batching plants
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): inserts after the fourth sentence of clause 10.2.5 the recommendation that, where batching plants are used, moisture content be determined by moisture probes fitted to the batching plants. The pre-amendment clause covered only the frequency-based-on-weather monitoring approach and the IS 2386 Part 3 reference for the determination method. The Amendment No. 4 insertion endorses inline probe-based moisture sensing as the preferred technique at batching plants. Standalone chunk because the probe recommendation is a discrete amendment-introduced rule with its own technology implication; the remainder of the clause (moisture compensation, IS 2386 Part 3, Table 10 fallback for nominal mixes only) is unchanged. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing moisture monitoring and water adjustment at the batching plant, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Surface Water Carried by Aggregate — categorical lookup by aggregate state
clauses.caption: Direct categorical lookup of approximate quantity of surface water carried by aggregate, indexed by aggregate state, in two equivalent units: percent by mass (column 3) and litres per cubic metre (column 4). Used per clause 10.2.5 for nominal mixes only when exact moisture data are unavailable, as a fallback estimate to drive the added-water adjustment. The four states are: very wet sand (highest surface water), moderately wet sand, moist sand, and moist gravel or crushed rock (lowest — coarse aggregate carries less surface water per unit mass than sand of similar wetness). Footnote: coarser the aggregate, less the water it will carry.
headers: Approximate Quantity of Surface Water — Percent by Mass
Approximate Quantity of Surface Water — l/m^3
clauses.note:
Footnote 1) — Coarser the aggregate, less the water it will carry. (Explains why moist gravel/crushed rock — coarsest of the four categories — has roughly half the surface water of moist sand at similar wetness, both in percent-by-mass and in l/m^3.)

Usage scope: Table 10 is invoked from clause 10.2.5 only for nominal mixes when exact moisture data are unavailable; design mixes are expected to use measured moisture content from the aggregate (per IS 2386 Part 3) rather than this fallback estimate.
clauses.nl_summary: Categorical lookup of approximate surface water carried by aggregate as a function of aggregate state (four categories from very-wet-sand to moist-gravel/crushed-rock), with equivalent values in percent-by-mass and l/m^3. Used for nominal-mix added-water adjustment when measured moisture data are unavailable.

variables.description: approximate quantity of surface water carried by the aggregate, expressed as a percentage of the aggregate mass (output, column 3)
approximate quantity of surface water carried by the aggregate, expressed in litres per cubic metre of aggregate (output, column 4; equivalent to column 3 under typical aggregate density)
aggregate state — one of {very_wet_sand, moderately_wet_sand, moist_sand, moist_gravel_or_crushed_rock} (input row axis)

clauses.notes: No amendments touch Table 10. The body of this 2021-reaffirmed PDF shows the table with the same four rows and the same two-column quantity layout encoded here; OCR of the printed PDF garbles the column-3 value for 'very wet sand' to '75' but the visually-verified value is '7.5' (consistent with the column-4 value of 120 l/m^3 under typical sand bulk density and with the descending sequence 7.5 → 5.0 → 2.5 → 1.25-2.5). The 1.25–2.5 range and 20–40 range in row iv) reflect that 'moist gravel or crushed rock' is a broader state category than the three sand states. No member-type tag applies — table is referenced from a construction-process clause and is itself a procedural lookup, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Mixing — mechanical-mixer mandate, IS compliance, and annual mixing-efficiency test
clauses.text:
Concrete shall be mixed in a mechanical mixer (see also IS 1791 and IS 12119).

It shall be ensured that stationary or central mixers and truck mixers shall comply with the performance criteria of mixing efficiency as per IS 4634. The mixing efficiency test shall be performed at least once in a year.

The mixers shall be fitted with water-measuring (metering) devices. The mixing shall be continued until there is a uniform distribution of the materials and the mass is uniform in colour and consistency. If there is segregation after unloading from the mixer, the concrete should be remixed.

(10.3.2) Workability should be checked at frequent intervals (see IS 1199).

The IS 4634 mixing-efficiency reference is the operative compliance check: the periodic test (at least annual) verifies that the actual mixer produces concrete meeting the spread-and-uniformity criteria of IS 4634, regardless of nameplate capacity or compliance with IS 1791 (concrete mixers — specification) or IS 12119 (general requirements for mixers). The water-metering-device mandate is a separate batching-control surface; the workability-check rule at 10.3.2 is a parallel verification that the mixed concrete meets its design workability before placement.
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): substitutes the first and second sentences of clause 10.3. The pre-amendment text was 'Concrete shall be mixed in a mechanical mixer. The mixer should comply with IS 1791 and IS 12119.' — a one-IS-compliance approach to mixer specification. The Amendment No. 4 substitution introduces the IS 4634 mixing-efficiency performance criterion and the once-yearly verification requirement, lifting the rule from equipment-specification compliance to performance-based compliance. The remainder of the clause (water-metering devices, mixing-until-uniform, remix-if-segregation) is unchanged. Bundles parent 10.3 (header + general mixing rules) with sub-clause 10.3.2 (workability checking) because 10.3.2 has no amendment of its own and reads as a short companion rule (one sentence + IS 1199 reference) to the mixing-uniformity discipline. The other 10.3 sub-clauses (10.3.1 mixing time, 10.3.3 admixture dosage) carry Amendment No. 4 substitutions and are split out as standalone chunks. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing mixing operations, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Mixing time — minimum two minutes for free-fall drum-type mixers
clauses.text:
As guidance, the mixing time shall be at least 2 minutes for conventional free-fall (drum) batch-type concrete mixers.

For other types of more efficient mixers, manufacturers' recommendations shall be followed.

The rule binds only the conventional drum-mixer case to the 2-minute floor; high-efficiency mixers (planetary, pan, twin-shaft) reach uniformity in shorter cycle times and are expected to follow the manufacturer's specification rather than a one-size-fits-all minimum.
clauses.notes: Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): full substitution of clause 10.3.1. The pre-amendment text was 'For guidance, the mixing time shall be at least 2 min. For other types of more efficient mixers, manufacturers' recommendations shall be followed; for hydrophobic cement it may be decided by the engineer-in-charge.' The Amendment No. 4 form (a) qualifies the 2-minute floor to free-fall drum batch-type mixers specifically (not all mixers in general) and (b) drops the hydrophobic-cement sub-rule (no longer carried in this clause; hydrophobic cement is covered as a permitted cement type in clause 5.1 with its own slower-strength-development handling in 5.1.1). Standalone chunk because the mixer-type-conditional rule is a distinct amendment-revised principle. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing mixing duration, applicable regardless of structural member type.

clauses.title: Admixture dosage limits — retarders, plasticizers, superplasticizers, and the polycarboxylate-specific ceiling
clauses.notes:
Reflects Amendment No. 4 (May 2013): full substitution of clause 10.3.3. This Amendment No. 4 substitution supersedes:
  (a) The original 2000-printed clause which read 'Dosages of retarders, plasticisers and superplasticisers shall be restricted to 0.5, 1.0 and 2.0 percent respectively by weight of cementitious materials and unless a higher value is agreed upon between the manufacturer and the constructor based on performance test.'
  (b) Amendment No. 2 (September 2005) which deleted the word 'and' from line 4 of the original clause — a small surgical edit that is fully subsumed by the Amendment No. 4 full substitution.
The Amendment No. 4 form (encoded here) adds the polycarboxylate-specific 1.0-percent ceiling (a substantive new rule covering the high-range water reducer class that became market-dominant between 2000 and 2013) and tightens the performance-test exception language to specify three named performance outcomes (workability, setting time, and early-age strength) — the pre-amendment text required only 'performance test' generically.
The final Amendment No. 4 form is the canonical version. No member-type tag applies — construction-process clause governing admixture dosing during mixing, applicable regardless of structural member type.
